author: Brian
title: Full of regret
comment_content: More calculus, polar coordinates, and some matrices.
comment_teaching: Skipped lectures, go read online tutorials.
comment_grading:
The 1014 final was on the 18th of May, 2016. It was also the day of the COMP2711 final.

Needless to say, the 18th of May was Judgement Day.

With each minute that passed in the exam hall, I could see fire rain down from the heavens and smite the poor souls, one by one. Given the conditions of the exam, it is highly unlikely that anyone would pass. I knew for a fact that I would fail. But I didn't.

Why?

There is only one possible explanation.

Ten days after the final exam and a few days before results were released, a divine tradegy occurred.

He died.
Almost noone noticed at first.
Reading the news gave me shivers.
After a while, I felt different.
My soul felt cleansed, my mind clear.
Boys and girls seemed worriless and carefree.
Everyone seemed.. purified.

He died for our sins. I will be forever grateful.

Rest in peace, our beloved savior.

author: happystar
title: MATH 1014 - a life lesson to learn, a memorable journey
comment_content:
When a boy first entered the classroom of MATH 1014, he believed it would be a manageable course.

"I have studied M2," he thought. His heart persuaded him that online comments saying it is difficult were because they haven't taken M2.

Days after days and mid-term exam passed,
One day when I was taking the bus, I saw the canvas notification, I knew, the date of judgment came. Click it, click it, wtf it kept loading,,,, loading....loading...and my heart was gradually falling... falling... and falling.
Suddenly, the screen turned red! The Canvas red! loading finish, and the score...

38/100

I couldn't believe it, was I a rubbish? oh sorry it was not a question. I was a rubbish. And it was my destiny to fail. Thank you UST, my dearest UST, it was you that gave me such a memorable experience that I would never forget. As a finance student, getting an F is equivalent to a failure, a loser... and my dream to pursue a Finance career is just like a joke...

I need to work hard. I knew it, but given the 35% mid-term weighting, any attempt seemed to be useless. I cried, I desperated, I shouted, I laugh, I... Maybe... I was emotionally collapsed.

I heard a lot of students were shouting or complaining at the forum, but I don't even have the motivation to shout. I tired...At a night around 2am, when I was once again despressed by what my destiny was. Suddenly, an email came...

THE GRADING SYSTEM CHANGED！

Mid term exam could be transferred to final! Suddenly, I smiled. I knew it was the God-given chance. I am not very bad at studying, and I knew it would be the only chance to prevent a F, which require 7 A+ if you still wish a dean list.

At that time, although it was at night full of darkness, I could feel a slight ray of hope was shinning on the sky, and waiting me to catch it.

I saw the Angel!

At the remaining time, I worked hard to fight for my grade. I knew, I really knew, that god won't give me a third chance. So the 90%-weighted final would be my only hope.

...
...
...

The exam ended, and I knew all the things gone, the toughness, the struggling, the effort... would be all become such a tiny little number, that would decide my whole destiny.

To be or not to be, that is a question.
To pass or not to pass, that decides my destiny.

At the end of the end, after clicking multiple times of canvas, my final score comes:

...

96/100

Suddenly, I was speechless. My voice was scrambling, and my eyes were staring at the screen.

Thank you MATH 1014 - you demonstrate a journey of life. From a talented M2 student to a fail student, and from a fail student to become a normal student again.

It is a simulation of a game of life

Where there is a will, there is a way...
comment_teaching: Teaching was generally OK, but I eventually found that the tutorial is better than the lectures. But anyway, it depends on self-study. Professors are just here to provide you with some materials for you to learn.

Remember, remember,
Where there is a will, there is a way.
Don't give up.

My score is not bad, but it seemed like the grading overall is bad. So I still give a C to represent the true situation of the course, rather than simply showing off my individual socre.

author: anonymousSheep
title: It's fun!!!! not......
comment_content:
If BDSM is your thing, then you'll enjoy this. You get to learn all sorts of ways to torture yourself you didn't even know existed!!! From the light and kinky stuff like volumes of revolution to the hardcore and masochistic stuff like series expansions and partial fractions expansions.

Okay, in all seriousness, it's Calculus 2 for those who follow the US curriculum. You learn integration techniques for the first half then you go on to learning series, sequences etc in the second half. I'm sure it's useful stuff, but still hard none the less.
comment_teaching:
Extremely difficult to understand what he's saying. He has about a 1:1 ratio of filler words to actual words when "teaching". So when he teaches it goes something like this "uhh, so uhhh, today we will ummm, be talking about uhhh integration by errrr parts. To uhhhh integrate........" When I heard that on the first class, I knew that I was in for a very bad time.

Basically non-existent teaching. Instructor blitz through the content like there's no tomorrow. If you go to class, you can only hope to learn something through osmosis, because you'll be furiously taking notes that you'll never read/won't understand or you'll give up half way and just start playing with your phone or flirting with the girl next to you. Which you can then flex your intelligence and show her how much you've self learned.

To rub salt into the wound, you get NDS students who seem to understand everything to the point where they can even correct the instructor's careless mistakes. fml. If they're that good, why even go to class??
comment_grading: Absolute bullshit. You get essentially a bi-modal distribution where you have us double digit IQ locals getting 30/100 and you have the 10% of students who say that this course was a breeze and get 90+/100 for everything. Mid term and final was a bloodbath
comment_workload: The only redeeming thing about this course is the workload. The only continuous assessment that is given is the infamous webwork. However, the webwork gives you unlimited tries so you can slowly guess your way through each assesment which takes only about 1.5-2 hours each on average. Or you could Wolfram Alpha that shit and be done in 15 minutes if you don't give a damn about learning the material.

author: ptrknow
comment_content:
我寫這個review是從沒有讀過M2的人的角度出發的，因為我自己也沒有讀過。如果要這個course的syllabus就請看其他review吧。

若然你沒有讀過M2(或微積分背景)，那這個course可以肯定是不會太容易的。以初次接觸而言，概念並非艱澀難懂，但可以算是很多(要我用英文來形容，就是overwhelming)。我用了相對地多的時間來學習以下的課題：

⭐Volume by slicing / disk / shell:
我自己很容易混淆:
何時使用disk? 何時使用shell?
從x-axis做in和從y-axis做in有什麼分別? 什麼時候要在x-axis做? y-axis呢?
若然旋轉軸不是x和y-axis呢?不同方法下，公式會有什麼不同呢?

⭐Work:
通常關於這個課題的題目會分開兩個部分。

第一個部分通常要求你找出裝在容器內的水的高度。需要用到DSE core的相似三角形和in volume的原理。

第二個部分其實就是套公式:
密度 x 加速度 x 距離 x (面積dx)
(面積dx) 其實就是體積，不過要用面積的公式來表達
至於interval就是由0到水的高度。

背後為何要這樣做，我花了不少的時間去理解。

⭐Integration by part:
我自己很常在這個課題出錯，不知道其他人如何了。

這個課題有個算是相當難的題型: prove reduction formula
當然不是叫你prove lnx 這麼低能。
詳情可以找zeta的練習來看看

⭐polar coordinate:
概念不難，公式不難，但題目可以很難
In area的題目可以讓你很頭疼。我要由哪裡In到哪裡?
這個課題更可以拼著volume一起考，請務必在pastpaper網找past paper來練練 (尤其以前014的，很值得做)

⭐sequence and series:
後面各種test不難，不要搞混就是

倒是前面一來就是一堆新的概念。一開始看著其實很茫然，但請花點時間消化和組織。你會發現他們並不是真的很困難。也是這一句：不要搞混就是

power series: 題型很死板，基本上就是要用到前面學到的各種test
要知道怎樣做d怎樣做In，要注意做d的時候第一個term"可能"會消失。

taylor series: 請自求多福。我現在也不敢說我很了解這個課題。

整體來看，內容其實還算有趣(很看人就是了)。
comment_teaching:
只講林子健和YIN GUO JIAN

林子健：
上了第一堂，當時課室人滿為患(你準時到達是要企的)
前半堂在說一些阿媽就是女人的話
說著說著，後半堂開始在解釋與1014無太大關係的東西
而且，他愈是解釋，我愈聽不懂...

YIN GUO JIAN:
也是上了第一堂
那個口音..世界級..沒幾句聽得明
notes呢?資源呢?叫我們找MSC去?
一張notes也沒有是怎樣? 而且連canvas都沒有
"What is canvas?" (YIN, 2018)

所以這個學期我基本上是自修的
而我自己用了
- textbook (裡面解釋得算很清楚，還有題目做練習呢。電子版本不算難找)
- ho hon ming notes (盡量找找source吧，有了它們會幫到你很多的)
- zeta notes (optional) : textbook練習和zeta練習可以二選一
- phyllis notes (optional) : 她有自己的example，附有zeta的練習
comment_grading:
有3個地方可以看出我們這個學期的midterm考得很差：
- B range門檻下降
- bonus quizzes
- final很簡單
所以整體而言，今個學期grading理應不算嚴謹

當然了，midterm我覺得還是算challenging
一來有時間限制，你要保持一定速度
二來長題目真的有點兒....
比如說有一題要In volume
那個圖形的公式有3個term，除了第一個term，其餘都是由各種e sinx cosx組成
我自己In了第一個term就走去做其他題目...
我知道要用by part來做，但並不代表我能(在限時內)做到..

聽講JIN TIAN LING是用"奇差"來形容我們的midterm成績😄

簡言之，要應付考試，請
- 盡量靠自己完成webwork，不要找wolfram alpha (1014的webwork比1012/1013的簡單多了)
- 平時多做練習 (書又好，zeta又好，其他tutor的都好)
- 臨考前盡量找past paper來做 (pastpaper網其實有很多，推薦做做以前014的，不用全做，找in-syl的，你覺得難的來做。當然的，現在1014的也要做，不用每份都做完，計不計時也由你)
- 考試時盡量保持頭腦清晰，避免犯低級錯誤

如果你的目標不高(e.g.隨便有個合格，或者b-range)，那其實題目隨便做做就行。但切忌臨考前囫圇吞棗，or you will fuck yourself up. 平時的溫習和練習對這科而言算是必要的

本人成績:
midterm: 85/100  final: 100/100  webwork: 100/100  bonus: 7/8
grade: A+
我不是來炫耀我的成績。我只是想告訴各位沒有讀過M2的人(尤其未來的學弟學妹們)，要超英趕美，並非不可能。

title: 0尊Professor, 害人course
comment_content:
Integration, series, polar
詳細請睇其他Po，因爲我主要黎分享1014既趣事

另外附上TKL既email，將會成爲經典建議大家背下：
Dear Math1014 students,

A number of email starting with “It is clear the overall midterm result of Math1014 ………” have reached my mailbox, but not as many as some students might have imagined; significantly less than 10 as far as I can remember. Given that about 150 students did not take the midterm exam, unlikely due to health problems, one could tell how confident some students were before the midterm day.

I do not find it necessary to argue about the level of difficulty of the midterm, and from a distribution point of view the midterm turns out to be somewhat normal; a bell-shaped instead of an L-shaped distribution. To put it on record, the past Math1014 exams before the rise of the all mighty ZOOM were all closed book exams, without calculator too.

Anyway, taking these unfortunate ZOOM days, or years, into account, I’ve unprecedentedly put a full solution of the midterm exam in the final exam information webpage. You probably should look at it and reflect on what went wrong with your own preparation for the midterm, especially if you fell apart in the midterm. Mumbling about how unfair the midterm was when compared with the past would not help a bit for passing the final. Still a few weeks of time to go before the end of the semester.

We are not here to bury students with Calculus, and there is no problem for us to switch into a max of two assessment scheme to release some pressure on students:

10%Homework + Max(35% midterm + 55% final,  90% final)  out of 100%

However, if you want a decent grade, you would have to work for it.

No assessment scheme can completely resolve the problems of those who are hit by anxiety. Don’t hesitate to seek professional help from the counselors in the Counseling and Wellness Center of HKUST if necessary.

https://counsel.hkust.edu.hk/

Regards,

-TK
comment_teaching:
太多精彩野，咁我由頭開始講起

第一件有趣既事發生係midterm果陣，我地既course coordinator TKL話如果中左肺炎就可以skip midterm只計final，但無中就鼓勵你去考，不過考衰左會照計埋你midterm :) 之後某討論區d人就水人唔好考midterm去送死，結果變左一班人無病都skip midterm

同一時間，呀紫珊瑚講明midterm同final唔會考reduction formula
"When I was a student (translation error), this (reduction formula) will not be included in the midterm or the final exam, because it is too complicated, I don't want you to spend 1 hour on doing this..." (Jishan HU, 2022) 結果midterm出左reduction formula，fake 9曬，一堆人無溫到唔識，搞到個mean唔過4成 (以往absolute grading 無40%係fail)，即係半班fail左要summer重讀，就算比你final考得好都一定拉唔翻高分，爛硬grade。

呢單野精彩到上埋連登，加上無考midterm班友係度笑9考左果班友，咁就引黎唔少人炸TKL email，要求兩個grading scheme (計or唔計midterm)。炎上到咁，TKL肯定比上頭照硬肺，照完超唔gurr，凌晨出左封email，語氣帶刺 "We are not here to bury students with Calculus" (TKL, 2022)

第二單精彩野就係紫珊瑚擺烏龍。先講少少背景，人地好細個就讀PhD in Applied Mathematics，所以希望大家如佢所講，“Don't underestimate your instructor's IQ" (Jishan HU, 2022)。拿拿拿，TKL封email講到明兩個grading scheme，跟住紫珊瑚話唔存在兩個scheme，得35%midterm 55%final果個，“Only Scheme 1 is used. I removed Scheme 2 since it is confusing. Scheme 2 was used for a reference only for the system.” (Jishan HU, 2022) 咁呀，d人之後拎住TKL封email對質，結果佢先跟翻兩個scheme，睇黎TKL真係有好好盡到coordinator既責任，竟然可以係出grade之前確保所有section用兩個grading system，好L感動。

好啦好啦，終於出grade，小弟就total 90.1拎A-既，本來就覺得純粹爛grade就算啦，好死唔死比我發現其他section 89.2有A grade。我OS：呀胡生， 90.1>89.2咁簡單既數唔洗學生教你呀嘛？你咁高IQ係咪先^^ 得得得，忍一時風平浪靜，我send封email問TKL發生咩事，佢話個excel檔未update我個分，係咪真係咁信下好啦，話唔定係咪每個section自己拉curve都唔知。TKL跟住就叫我揾紫珊瑚更正個grade，我email佢，佢就遲遲唔肯覆email。最好笑係我一forward埋比TKL，紫珊瑚幾分鐘内幫我update個grade。好啦更正翻個grade，大團圓結局了。

係肺炎期間online上堂，竟然能夠比一個咁精彩，咁多花生食既course比我，TKL受少少氣都值得既，我代替班學生黎多謝你，仲有bye bye你條尾，係咁多~

正如一開始所言，當所有人都共同站在一起的時候改變才會發生。港校的GPA制度已讓人詬病已久，在過去20多30年世界普遍沒GPA Inflation的時候，平均2.x的GPA並不會阻止你申請研究生，當然大多數人會選擇直接工作，那GPA就更加不重要了。但進入21世紀20年代不少人都希望能在海外繼續進修，而在學歷通脹的背景下甚至連面試企業都要看你的GPA。大部分內地、美國以至是英國學校在GPA寬鬆了不少，而與此同時香港的大學仍然沒有跟上步伐，在可見的將來，所謂精英的結果不會有太大改變，但對於GPA在2:2左右的學生，這樣的改變是災難性的，我已經聽說不少人僅僅因為GPA不達標而被各類工作升學機會淘汰，這點十分可惜，大學原先是你人生的墊腳石，如今糟糕的GPA卻成了你人生的障礙。希望每位普通學生勇於發聲，要求學校改革現有的GPA以至是教學制度。Stay Tuned.

title: How Not to Run a Course: A Brief Guide
comment_content:
Content is fine. One of the more positive things about this course.

Integration Techniques: Integration by Parts, Trigonometric Substitution, Partial Fractions
Numerical Approximation of Integrals
Applications of Integrals: Arc Length and Surface Area of Revolution, Average Value of Function, Work Done
Improper Integrals
Polar Coordinates: Polar Curves, Slopes and Arc Length of Polar Curves
Series and Sequences: Convergence Testing and Sums of Infinite Series, Maclaurin and Taylor Series
Vectors (Excluded in final as they ran out of time)

Some of the content might be useful in the future.

Midterms covered up to Work Done. Topics tested in the LQs are reduction formulae and solids of revolution.

LQ in the finals included solids of revolution, convergence of series, and reduction formulae (again).
comment_teaching:
If the content is the more positive thing about this course, then the teaching, and administration, are utter trainwrecks.

If we purely talk about the teaching of the course material, TK Lam actually did an OK job, despite frequent miscalculations. The most irritating thing is the attitude of the administration of this course.

After the midterm (the arrangements of which you can read from the Grading section), students complained that the grading scheme unfairly favours those who skipped the midterm. TK Lam responded by emailing us the following:
1. Saying the midterm is not particularly difficult when compared to past years, and the distribution is bell curved;
2. "Unprecedentedly" putting the midterm solutions up (shouldn't this be your responsibility anyways? "Instructors should.... Provide timely and constructive formative feedback to students on their performance as an important part of the student learning process." (Academic Registry, 2022));
3. Blaming students for not working hard; and
4. Telling us to seek professional counseling.

Apart from being unnecessarily rude and very condescending, it shows a total disregard of students who may be suffering due to insufficient MATH background. Students with C/D in 1012 might be forced to enrol in this course as it is a program requirement. Some other reviewers who quoted TKLam and wrote, "There are no excuses apart from laziness." should consider that the world does not revolve around them, and everyone's circumstances are different.

Another egregious example of administrative failure can be readily seen in the poor coordination between instructors. One instructor mentioned nearly verbatim the reduction formulae question in midterm. However, he immediately dismissed students' concerns by saying "this will not be examined in the midterm and final examination because it's too time consuming". The question appeared nearly exactly in the midterm. This highlights the failure in coordination and management of this course.

The instructor of my tutorial, Phyllis, provided useful notes and exercises. Her teaching is the only positive thing I can say about the teaching of this course.
comment_grading:
If you consider the mean of the examinations, most of the lecture sections will have mean C or D. Most famously, L2 (TK Lam's section) got a failing mean for the midterm.

The grading scheme was originally as follows:
10% Homework, 35% Midterm, 55% Final

Before the midterm, there were several threads on Goop (a social media platform targeted towards local higher ed students) about their unpreparedness for the midterm. Some anonymous people commented that they should skip the midterm if they are not prepared enough, as the midterm percentage will be automatically transferred to the final.

After the midterm, "around 150 students" (TK Lam, 2022) are found to have skipped the (more difficult) midterm. As one lecturer who wishes to remain anonymous mentioned, skipping the midterm to get a better grade should never have been an option without medical proof. How is it fair that students are allowed to skip major course grading components freely without repercussions?

After the midterm, the following appeared in the final webpage:
"No makeup exam will be arranged for minor medical reason, even with a letter form a clinical doctor. Exceptions will only be made if you are hospitalized with proof."

One can only note that TK Lam is a remarkably "compassionate" professor.

The new grading scheme, after complaints detailed in "Teaching", are as follows:
10% Homework + max(35% midterm and 55% final, and 90% final only)

A final anecdote: The instructor who gave away the midterm question previously (in the "Teaching" section) removed the 2nd grading scheme because it was "too complicated". Although this was swiftly remedied, it again highlights the incompetence in management and administration.
comment_workload:
Workload is moderate, as per MATH course standards.

The only workload is revision for both exams, and completing the (unlimited tries) homework.

Do get full marks in every homework, as you'll need something to save your grade after midterms and finals.

Maybe the main workload is dealing with the constant load of bullshit.

comment_content:
- Integration techniques: fundamental theorem of calculus, substitution rules, trigonometric identities and trigonometric integrals, integration by parts, partial fractions, numerical integration, etc..
- Improper integrals: evaluation, convergence and divergence problems.
- Applications of integrals: areas, volumes by slicing and cylindrical shells, arc lengths and areas of surfaces of revolution, work done, average value of a continuous function, calculus in polar coordinates.
- Infinte sequences and series: limit of an infinite sequence, convergence and divergence an infinite series, geometric series, various convergence/divergence tests, alernating series.
- Power series: interval of convergence and radius of convergence of a power series, Taylor and Maclaurin series of functions, operations of power series, such as adding, substituting, differentiating or integration power series.
- Vectors (WAS NOT IN FINAL DUE TO CORONA SEMESTER)

comment_content:
I highly recommend taking L1 to those who are interested in mathematics and don't mind putting more time and effort in this course. The content is same as that of Ivan IP's 1014 L1, so I won't be listing it. Bascially it is more rigourous and proof based compared to normal section.

So, even if I, a non L1 guy, understood it then 1013L1 and 1023 guys should have no problem.
But the exams are much more tedious ( and the answers& proofs also will look hideous) compared to that of Ivan's. As there is much more computational stuff and henry tries to test each and every thing that he has taught in this course.
comment_grading:
Grading is far far better than normal section. In fact, I think I will get higher grade in 1014 L1 than, what I got in 1013 non L1(B+). Now that the finals marks have been released. But don't take it just because the grading is better, you will absolutely suffer if you don't like maths. Some of my friends who got A- in 1013 non L1 got cooked (below LQ).
The only complaints that I have is some TAs won't give comments on the assignemnts (they just deduct marks and expect us to improve and one time they even gave me zero for a completely valid proof just because it was different from the marking scheme)
For refernce:
Midterms:
n = 131
Mean = 40.20, Standard deviation = 19.17
Max = 90, upper quartile = 53.5, median = 36.5, lower quartile = 26.
My score = 26.5/100
Finals:
n = 126
Mean = 40.36, Standard deviation = 20.41
Max = 92.5, upper quartile = 57.5, median = 37.5, lower quartile = 23.5
My score = 61/100
Also, the grading scheme is very generous, you get to choose the highest out of the two: 30%midterms + 20% HWs + 50% midterms or,10% midterms + 20% HWs + 70% Finals
As you can see, the difference between my midterms vs finals is purely a result of consistency. I will quote Hhm here: "Don't give up!"
comment_workload: Workload depends on your mathematical maturity, and your familiarity with summation/integral manipuation etc. And if you have 0 mathematical maturity(just plug and chug formula) like me, then no worries, you will learn and develop some basic skills throughout this course. While you do learn a ton of stuff, It will take you literally dozen of hours to finish & understand just a single question. It took me 28 hrs to finish assignment 1. Even the simplest trick would require me 1 or 2 hours to figure out. But after you have figured out the trick, It will be engrained in your memory. So, expect to work hard. Even though there maybe just 8 assignments per semester.

author: SQ Flyer
title: Hopefully applications more than theoretical & thinks like mathematicians
comment_content:
A continual of MATH 1013 and the Calculus Course Series for all Engineering, Mathematics, RMBI and some Science/ Business students. Focus more on applications of calculus than pure theories and theorems that only mathematicians are likely to enjoy playing with them and have such a "pure" mindset.

Topics include
Applications of integration (Area b/ curves, Volumes using Shell/Disk/Washer/Slicing Method, arc lengths, area of surface of revolution, avg. value of function, Work)

Integration Techniques (By Parts, Trigo Integral, Trigo Sub.,Partial Fractions, Numerical Integrations using 3 rules, Improper Integrals)

Polar Coordinates and Calculus (transformation, curves plotting, arc length, slope and area)

Sequences and Series (Limit Theorem, Whole Bunch of Tests, Power Series,Taylor Series and Maclaurin Series)

Vectors(Dot/Cross Product with mixed triple product)

The course focus more on applications of calculus rather than rigorous mathematical proofs and playing with pure mathematics.

The topics are in great variety and some of the topics are not actually useful for students with different intended majors and backgrounds. The whole course I think only the integrations are necessary for all students, the vectors are just for Civil/Mech/Sci students, Series mostly for EE/CE/Sci students.
comment_teaching:
The teaching styles vary between instructors.

For LI Jun, she performed below standard. Her class is like direct copying all the stuffs from the textbook and read aloud repeatedly without further explanations, implications and conclusions.The examples shown in class are usually way too easy to understand and it's just not the exam level examples. She focus a lot on sub-topics that are actually not the key point of the chapters. And her accent,typical Mainland accent and sometimes unable to pronounce words correctly.

For TK Lam, he did a better job, just better but not the best. At least he is familiar with the topics covered in this course because he is in charge of the course for decades. However, sometimes he may cover examples which are not commonly found in the textbook and some topics are actually quite difficult to understand by just read the textbook. He addressed those parts with limited examples and not explaining in a very clear manner.Furthermore, he covers the topics in a fast manner and rush for completing all the content due to limited lecture time. And his writing... I think most of you know he writes in a messy and unorganized manner, sometimes he did mistakes and spent a lot of time searching the root of mistakes due to his terrible writing.
He is not that god-like but still just fine.

For Phyllis, the specialized IA of TK Lam, did a much better job. Her tutorial notes and exercises are useful and help students to organize and consolidate the course content well. author: Duqq
title: A terrible course which does not reward studying in my opinion (with this prof)
comment_content:
I feel very sorry for people that are required to take this course, being everyone.

Think about the smartest kid in your math class, they can get an A. I mean the kid that goes to math competitions, genuinely loves math and can spend all day thinking about math. That is what's required to get an A in this course in my opinion.

I always thought math was one of my strongest subjects until I took this course. I did a Math EE for IB and was able to consistently score 6's and 7's in HL AA Mathematics.

Despite being the course that I studied for 3x more than my other courses, it ended up being my worst grade. (B-). Don't get me wrong, it's not impossible to get an A, it's just very very very very very very very difficult.

The tutorial guy is very nice though which is a plus.
Format is Calc II material.
comment_teaching:
CHIANG, Edmund is truly an interesting teacher. If you enjoy math you will genuinely enjoy this course. However, if you simply want to get a good grade, I wish you luck.

His teaching takes a pure approach to the theories being taught in class. It is very methodical and well thought out. Except for the fact that you can't understand half of what he is saying if you do not have a strong background in proofs. Furthermore, his lectures sometimes deviate from the course material purely for his own interest. He claims "you guys must know about this, this is the most interesting modern approach to mathematics there is". Only to say at the end of the lecture that it isn't required for the course.

So anyways, I hate this professor honestly, it's simply not the way I wish to be taught Math. I skipped all lectures after he began deviating from the course material (lecture 9?) and just learned from the organic chemistry tutor on YT.
comment_grading:
Weighting:
25% midterm, 15% HW, 60% Finals

HW is easy because you have infinite attempts so not getting 100% on the HW will put you behind.

Mid-term is maybe 100% easier than the final, because the test is open-book you simply need to print out the lecture notes and it's 25% of the test for free probably?  It's still difficult though.

Final is also open book, but it won't help. Because our dear professor loves the pure side of Calc II so much that he decided to change the format of the final test. I completed all the previous past papers of MATH1014 and was able to score 80% timed. On the actual final I got 44%. Why? Because the questions being asked were not similar at all to all 3 past papers. Completely a different approach to testing students. Which in my opinion, is stupid because you should be rewarded for studying past papers? no? Maybe it's just an IB thing.

Anyways in my opinion if you get CHIANG, Edmund, you should attend every lecture and genuinely try to keep up with what he's saying, that's the best bet at getting a good grade in this course.

Except for the lectures where he teaches things that are irrelevant to the course, which you won't know until he tells you at the end of the lecture so... yeah.

ALSO:
Grading is absolute I've heard, so standard deviation whatever doesn't matter.

HW:
Mean = 75.38%

Mid-term
Mean = 48.6%

Final
Mean = 37.63%

Total:
Mean = 47.3%      SD=18.4%
Me: 57% = B-

Here is exactly how the grading works:
1.) they determine the mean after all exams are done
2.) they make the mean a high C+
3.) everything else is relatively graded based off this mean. +0.5 sd is B-, +1.0 sd is B-B+, +2SD is A range? I wouldn't know.
comment_workload:
12 HW, requiring maybe ~2.5 Hours per HW to complete, maybe less. It depends if you need to learn the content the HW requires or if you already know it, also it starts easy and gets much harder once they begin teaching sequences, infinite series, conversion, and a bunch of tests to see where series converge/diverge.

moderate - intensive. Definitely one of the courses which required the most time from the ones I took.

author: mark_
title: only take in fall semester if you have a real interest in maths
comment_content:
Quite interesting for someone interested in maths.

The course becomes extremely theoretical in the second half of the semester, so be warned. If you are not used to this kind of maths, mentally prepare yourself.

Content:
- Area between curves, different methods of calculating volumes of revolution, arc lengths, surface area of revolution.
- Work done, Hooke's law, Newton's Second Law (why is this in a MATH course?)
- Integration techniques: Integration by parts, trigonometric/hyperbolic integrals and substitutions, reduction formulas.
- Polar curves (this is hard to grasp, so study ahead!), polar arc lengths/areas, polar parametrizations.
- Approximate integration (trapezium, midpoint, Simpsons')
- Improper integreals
- Sequences, convergence, test for convergence, epsilon-N definition of a limit - basically the first half of MATH1023
- Power series, Taylor series, radius of convergence
- Basic vectors (why is this in a calculus course? you don't even do any vector calculus)

The content is interesting but there are things you should definitely study ahead for. Some content is hard to grasp if you don't have great mathematical intuition. I would recommend studying up on polar curves and the epsilon-delta and epsilon-N definition of a limit.
comment_teaching:
The professor is clearly extremely passionate about mathematics and teaching. This is especially evident when he throws in some maths trivia/history in the teaching, and also tries to show us the deeper meaning of some things.

However, he often fails to explain concepts clearly to begin with, and they only make sense if you have some surface-level understanding of the concepts. His explanations are also quite quick, so you have to pay close attention. He also makes lots of mistakes, which people often point out.


This professor is clearly passionate though, as mentioned earlier. After one lecture, I asked him a question about the complex form of the trigonometric functions and whether I could use those to my advantage in exams. After answering my question, he took me outside for a 30 minute one-to-one lecture on the history of maths and mathematicians/physicists/philosphers like Newton and Ramanujan and their contributions. It was interesting but certainly uncalled for.

The TA (Tommy) is very good according to my classmates. I agree to an extent but I found his teaching somewhat boring, but he taught some really useful manouevers which definitely helped in the exams. You should attend his tutorials.
comment_grading:
Webwork (homework) - 15%
Midterm exam - 25%
Final exam - 60%

For those just wanting to know what the grading is like with this prof, it's like this based on data from people around me.
Mean => C
+0.9SD => B
+2.1SD => A
(these scores are enough to get you that grade. they are not the cutoff or anything)

Stats:
Webwork - mean is somehow 80-85%????
Midterm: Mean 33.5/70, SD 17.1
Final: Mean 39.10/100, SD 18.8
Overall: Mean 48.23/100, SD 18.4
(note: the mean for the final and overall score are up to date but the updated SD has not yet been provided)

You should get full marks on the Webwork because you get unlimited attempts. Some questions are tricky though, so don't leave it to the last minute.

The midterm is moderately difficult. The main difficulty is the time limit, so try and speed through easier questions and find clever shortcuts if you can.

The final is EXTREMELY DIFFICULT. There were questions on my final that I would suspect most people cannot do. It is especially difficult for people who try and follow a rigid formula for working out similar categories of questions. For some questions, you need to be more flexible.

There was also one 3 mark question which was completely out of syllabus and could only be solved by referring to a very specific proof in the lecture notes. Oh, and I shouldn't forget to mention that proof had many bugs. The max score for the final was 97/100, so one could guess that even the top scorer could not solve that problem.

My grades:
Webwork: 100%
Midterm: 55/70
Final: 80/100

Overal: 82.64% (grade A)
comment_workload:
Extremely light.

11 WebWorks, each with a few questions. You can sit down and do these when you are bored, because you get unlimited attempts so there is no risk of losing out on points. Some are difficult though (especially homework 10) so if you do them at the last minute, you might not finish on time.

However, you should study for the midterm and finals. As mentioned, some questions are extremely difficult, and doing some problems from the textbook and sample exams will definitely help.

comment_teaching:
prof全程手寫notes，像在施展“鬼畫符大法”。那些筆記彎彎曲曲，龍飛鳳舞，遠看像天書，近看像抽象派作畫。

我上堂認真聽講奈何速度過快，落堂將notes喂給AI識別——結果AI直接崩潰：“抱歉，此乃亂碼，非人類語言。”。

連OCR都認不出他在寫什麽，我一個散修怎麽跟得上？只好全程靠猜，猜錯了繼續猜，猜到最後直接放棄，靠自學Youtube野路子。
comment_grading:
功課mean苦苦支撐，midterm mean勉强續命，Final直接2分暴擊（可能用脚踩幾下ans sheet都有幾分）

從開課第一日起，我就知道這學期是一場硬仗。
prof書寫速度快如閃電，筆在notes上舞出“劍氣橫縱”的殺陣，每一筆都像心魔在嘲笑我靈根太差。
筆記？不存在的。那是前輩高人留下的“殘卷秘籍”，可惜殘得太徹底，連AI都看不懂，只能靠我自己意會神交。
上課成了練心之旅：一邊看著notes發吽哣，一邊默念“心靜自然涼”，結果心沒靜，涼意直入丹田。

然後，2025年12月19日——exam period的終極閉關日。
沒有其他科目分擔火力，全宇宙只剩這門課在對我放天雷。
走進考場，劫雲密佈，雷霆隱隱作響。
題目一出，直接九九八十一難現形：前半段似曾相識，後半段劍陣啓動，直刺我空蕩蕩的元神。
我真氣逆行，靈識渙散，眼看就要被轟成飛灰，魂魄打入畜生道（Fail）。
全場修士皆面如死灰，這最後一劫，無人可渡。
考完後，我抱著「期中作業 mean，應該能苟個及格」的最後一絲仙念，閉關等成績。
12月31日，成績如天道無情詔書降臨——期末：2
總分:Fail

那一瞬，靈台炸裂，業障反噬，三魂七魄當場離體。
原來那些看不懂的手寫 notes，不是教學，而是「滅頂預言」。
期中 mean、作業 mean，像兩顆勉強煉成的下品護身符，在天雷面前瞬間灰飛煙滅。

我終於悟道：
天道無常，不認 mean 值。
沒有散修前輩替我擋雷。
沒有隱世高人羽化渡劫。
它精準劈中了我這個「連 AI 都看不懂 notes」的廢柴散修。
從此對 MATH1014 只有四個字：服了，認栽。
下輩子投胎，求個 OCR 天賦滿級 + 靈根 A+，再來戰這破微積分。

願還在煉丹的師弟師妹，早日破解老師的鬼畫符，渡劫飛升。
我？已元神俱滅，道號：零分符籙散修。
R.I.P. my GPA

lectures are mostly him explaining theories that gives you ZERO help when it comes to examination.

Tutorials aren't useful. Stop attending after the first 2 weeks. But it's useful if you want to ask questions.

Attendance is not a thing. So do whatever you want.
comment_grading:
mid-term is mediocre. difficulty and style are just right and very similar to sample paper that is given by the professor. Make sure to understand every question when doing the sample paper. it helps alot.

Final exam is literally the definition of "hell". so accurate that it should be written into the oxford dictionary.

Smart-looking dude sitting next to me stopped writing after 10 minutes and proceeded to scratch his head for the next 1hr 50 minutes.

I suppose that it's beacuse prof Chiang teaches all lessons and he is the only one writing the paper. It's nothing like sample paper.

In general: VERY poor grades. you should be celebrating if you passed this course in one piece.
comment_workload:
Depends.

the actual workload is WebWork. Very little questions (around 15 for every assignment.). Not difficult and you should really be aiming to take all the marks available aka 100%. This is the only chance to boost your grade. Forget about scoring top marks in exam.

however, if you really do want better grades (B or B+ above), make sure to do LOADS of practice. in the book Calculus by J.Steward, you get plenty of practice questions. Try to revise every day or at least every week. Too much to understand.

I swear if i dont have that study break before final , I shall see you all next semester.

author: L7E2O9
title: Wolfram Alpha saves your life, fuck grading system
comment_content:
I can sum up the content as follows:
1. Review of integration
2. Applications of integration, including area between 2 curves, volume, shell method, cylindrical shell method, etc......
3. arc lengths, surface area of revolution, work
4. convergence and divergence
5. infinite sequences and series (divergence test, integral test, comparison test, ratio test and root test)
6. series of functions (Taylor series and Maclaurin series)
7. Vectors (not covered in final)

If you are interested in digging deeper in math, this course is perfect. If not, this course will screw up your GPA.

Final:
part a: 36/36 (mean: 24.29/36)
part b: 16/32 (mean: 19.62/32)

author: matt au
title: what the hell is this course
comment_content:
I'm writing this review from the perspective of a normal university student who got in with average grades (DSE core math 4, never studied m1 or m2).

Math 1012 was already hard enough for me as the lessons progressed really fast and all of the content was new to me (got C).

Did all the past papers before the exam and only got a C-.

Just shows how hard this course is
comment_teaching:
Never went to lectures or tutorials. Can't say anything else except all the lecturers are mainlanders and therefore have a heavy mainland accent. Impossible to understand what they are talking about.

Powerpoints and lecture notes are also mostly useless as they contain next to no useful information such as equations, only some vague question types that have no answers.

You are much better off binge-watching organic chemistry tutor videos on every topic at 2x speed, or learning with AI.
comment_grading:
Absolute dumpster fire of a grading system.

The main problem with this course as a student from math 1012 is that you have to compete with students who have studied m1/m2 before in high school (aka math 1013). It is impossible to compete with them and get a good grade unless you work your ass off and have no life

Granted, this course is based on absolute grading where >85% is A, >70% is B, >55% is C, >40% is D, and anything lower is an F.

HOWEVER, mean is usually C+ in this course, which means if you get ~70% (which might seem like a good grade), you're still getting a B-.

For reference, 5 marks off the mean is a C+.

Thank god there is a slight adjustment of the grading so you might get the grade if you are +-2 from the cutoff
comment_workload:
Have 10 webworks in the whole semester but nothing too annoying if you use AI to do it.

Just buy a yearly subscription of POE for 1599 hkd for 1 million points per month and blitz through that shit in 15 minutes (and only start learning 1 week before the exam)

author: gbean
title: Hard but not impossible
comment_content:
Every topic covered in the course has to do with integral calculus.

Topics I can remember:
- Finding area and volume of shapes with multiple methods (solids of revolution / cylindrical shells etc)
- Finding arc lengths and surface area of 3d shapes
- Application of integrals: physical quantities such as displacement and work in Physics
- Integration methods: integration by parts, trig substitutions, partial fractions
- Numerical integration, error bounds
- Polar coordinates
- Improper integrals
- Infinite series and sequences
- Taylor / Mauclarian series.

I'd say the content is quite organized.
Don't understand something? Memorize all the formulas. At least that's how I survived.
comment_teaching:
This professor spends a lot of time going through practice questions after explaining the basic concepts. He likes to do a lot of proving of concepts for students who are interested. (I did not pay attention to this part because they are really complex and don't show up in the exams).

He uploads his class notes and lectures to Canvas so you can catch up if you miss any lectures.
comment_grading:
No attendance policy for both lectures and tutorials.

I believe the percentage was
Homework 15% + Midterm 35% + Final 50%.

For homework you have unlimited attempts, so keep spamming answers until you get it right. I don't think I lost any credit for having 30+ attempts for 1 question.

I got full marks for homework, 84% on midterm, 76% on final. My letter grade was A-. I was expecting a lower grade so I was quite happy with what I got.

An ideal way to get a good grade would be reviewing the basic concepts, memorizing the formulas that are not given on the formula sheet (there are quite a lot), then going through the tutorial questions and/or past papers.
I was quite scared because the tutorial questions and past papers were much harder than the homework questions, but I believe the midterm and final for this semester was easier than the previous years' past papers. If you know how to solve the hardest problems from the tutorials, you should do pretty well on the exam.
comment_workload: Homeworks are posted on webwork, with due dates coming up about once every 2 weeks. If you understand the material properly, I believe you can finish each assignment under 2 hours. For me it usually took more because I had to go back and understand the concepts before actually solving the questions.

After hosting a mt exam whose mean lower than 50, TKLAM wrote: (strongly recommend to recite all this cause it could be classic)
A number of email starting with “It is clear the overall midterm result of Math1014 ………” have reached my mailbox, but not as many as some students might have imagined; significantly less than 10 as far as I can remember. Given that about 150 students did not take the midterm exam, unlikely due to health problems, one could tell how confident some students were before the midterm day.

I do not find it necessary to argue about the level of difficulty of the midterm, and from a distribution point of view the midterm turns out to be somewhat normal; a bell-shaped instead of an L-shaped distribution. To put it on record, the past Math1014 exams before the rise of the all mighty ZOOM were all closed book exams, without calculator too.

Anyway, taking these unfortunate ZOOM days, or years, into account, I’ve unprecedentedly put a full solution of the midterm exam in the final exam information webpage. You probably should look at it and reflect on what went wrong with your own preparation for the midterm, especially if you fell apart in the midterm. Mumbling about how unfair the midterm was when compared with the past would not help a bit for passing the final. Still a few weeks of time to go before the end of the semester.

We are not here to bury students with Calculus, and there is no problem for us to switch into a max of two assessment scheme to release some pressure on students:

10%Homework + Max(35% midterm + 55% final,  90% final)  out of 100%

However, if you want a decent grade, you would have to work for it.

No assessment scheme can completely resolve the problems of those who are hit by anxiety. Don’t hesitate to seek professional help from the counselors in the Counseling and Wellness Center of HKUST if necessary.

comment_grading:
10% HW
35% Midterm
55% Finals

OR

10% HW
90% Finals

This course adopts the absolute grading scheme which is bullshit in my honest opinion. For a course as difficult as this, adopting a curved grading scheme is the most rational thing to do. The fact that the midterm mean was very low (3x-4x) means that almost half the class failed or are nearing a D range grade. And what if you get +2 SD above the mean? Congratulations!!! You might get a B+ or a B. Wow.

My friends from other universities said their Calculus II classes were reasonable. Simply getting above the mean will guarantee a B at the very least. And for my friends whose Calc 2 class adopts the absolute grading scheme, their professor was reasonable in setting up the exam papers and was lenient in marking.

In conclusion, this course is a shitshow.

author: Oscarlee
comment_content:
Calculus II topics include:
1) Integration Techniques:
- Basic Calculus Review (5.2-5.5)
- Integration by parts [1013 covered] (7.1)
- Partial fractions (7.4)
- Trig Integration (7.2)
- Trig Substitution (7.3)
2) Area & Volume:
- Area under Curves (6.1)
- Washer / Disk Method (6.2)
- Cylindrical Shells Method (6.3)
- Volume of revolution, revolution of surface area, arc length
3) Polar coordinates and calculus:
- Polar coordinates & Polar Equations
- Slopes of tangent
- Area & Volume in Polar Graphs
- Arc lengths & Surface of Revolution
4) Sequence & Series:
- Arithmetic / Geometric Series / Sequence
- Telescoping Series
- Alternating Series
- Improper Integrals (7.8)
- Power Series (11.8-11.9)
- Taylor & Maclaurin Series (11.10)
- Taylor Polynomials (11.11)
5) Convergence & Divergence of series:
- Divergence Test (11.4)
- Comparison Test (11.4)
- Limit Comparison Test (11.4)
- Alternating Series Test (11.5)
- Integral Test (11.3)
- Ratio & Root tests (11.6)

IB Math AA HL would be very helpful. The content is rather okay if you already have a background, but you still need to revise to at least secure a B. Good luck if you are new to all the topics here. You will suffer a lot.

author: RJ
comment_content:
Week 1 - Definite Integrals, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Indefinite Integrals, Substitution Rule, Area Between Curves
Week 2 - Volume of Revolution
Week 3 - Applications of Integrals: Work, Average Value of a Function
Week 4 - Integration by Parts, Trigonometric Integration
Week 5 - Trigonometric Substitution
Week 6 - Integration by Partial Fractions, Approximate Integration
Week 7 - Improper Integrals, Arc Length, Surface Area of Revolution
Week 8 - Polar Coordinates & Calculus
MIDTERMS!
Week 9 - Infinite Sequences, Infinite Series
Week 10 - Integral Test, Comparison Test, Alternating Series
Week 11 - Absolute Convergence, Ratio Test, Root Test, Power Series, Representation of Functions as a Power Series
Week 12 - Taylor & Maclaurin Series, Applications of Taylor Polynomials
FINALS!

There's also final lessons about Vectors, Dot Product, Cross Product that we didn't manage to get to, and thus it was not on the finals. I have heard that previous classes didn't reach the end as well, so most likely you might not get this lesson, but for those planning to take math courses past MATH1014, you'll probably need to study this independently.
comment_teaching: The teacher I managed to get is relative newcomer Stephen Liu. In my opinion, he is a very good teacher. Firstly, he knows the material very well, since he is well-spoken and can explain topics in a digestible manner. He also has well-written notes that are always published in Canvas a day or two after. They are so detailed that if you can't attend one of his classes, the lecture notes provide all the information you need. He is also very open to student queries via email. For students who struggle in math, he might be a suitable fit for you, the only downside I can think of is that it might be hard for some students to catch up with the pace he teaches.
comment_grading:
Homework (WebWork) - 10%
- Just finish answering them before the deadline and you'll be fine

Midterms - 35%
- Mean: 49.14 out of 100

Finals - 55%
- Mean: 53.32 out of 100

(The curve will be higher in fall semester because the course will be filled with good math kids. The competition should be less during spring)

For both midterms and finals, the first half (50-60%) consists of about 15 multiple choice questions. More often than not, they are mostly direct applications to what you've learned, with some deviations that require more problem-solving skills and confidence in your math knowledge. The second half consists of 2 to 4 "show your solutions" questions. These are segmented into different parts, which most of the time are connected. Sometimes they delve deeper into more critical thinking stuff, but most of the time it's basically the multiple choice questions but instead of answering abcd you write down your solution. Just review the past papers uploaded in Canvas, since they follow the question styles very closely.

The satisfaction on the grading system really depends on how confident you are in scoring the mean. If you are below average in math you'll think it's too hard, and if you are a math wizard you'll think it's too easy. I scored pretty well above the mean, so I'm alright with the grading system.
comment_workload: The only workload required for this course are the MATH1014 homework found in WebWork. They appear once a week, and require nothing but inputting the correct answer. It's basically normal homework.

Most of the content was learned in high school (if you were taking DSE M2 or international courses), but it's not friendly for M1 students (because I'm studying M1). If you want to choose a course related to a math major, you have to take this course, which is unfortunate.

Keyword Syllabus:
• Review of definite integrals and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
• Integration by parts, trigonometric integrals, trigonometric substitutions, polar coordinates and calculus, partial fractions.
• Numerical integration
• Improper integrals.
• Area of a region between curves
• Volume by the methods of slicing and cylindrical shells.
• Length of curves, surface area, work, and the average value of a function.
• Sequences and infinite series, divergence and integral, ratio, root, and comparison tests, alternating series.
• Taylor polynomials, power series, and Taylor series.
• Vectors in two and three dimensions, dot products, cross products.

title: Nice course content but do work hard
comment_content:
Approximate schedule and syllabus:
- Review of definite integrals and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. (Covered in MATH1013)

- Improper integrals

- Volume by the methods of slicing and cylindrical shells

(Final coverage up to this point, cumulative)

Everything else is not difficult to handle, as the questions and concepts are usually similar across all papers. Vectors are not included in this course, but will be included in MATH2011/2023 (Multivariable Calculus), thus for those who are taking the respective courses, either for interest or program requirements, self-studying vectors is essential.

hw:full midterm:96 final:91 total: 93.65—A+
Although I get A+ in this course, I won’t say it is a course which gives really good grades. It seems A+ grades aren’t given out too liberally, while the cutoff for A and A- might be slightly lower, making them more attainable. That said, you’ll still need to put in solid effort. I’ll break down how to approach the course effectively in the ‘Workload’ section.
comment_workload:
Since I overloaded this semester, I couldn’t dedicate as much time to this course as I’d have liked. If you’re taking MATH 1014 actively—not just as a core requirement—I’d strongly recommend making sure you’ve truly mastered integration from 1013 first. Without that foundation, the course can feel overwhelming. Personally, I stayed afloat by keeping up with lectures at 1.5x speed and never falling behind on assignments. About a week before the midterm , I worked through problems from the Mainland textbook for deeper practice, then switched to timed past papers. This routine paid off—I finished the actual  midterm exam 25 minutes earlier. One critical lesson was prioritizing conceptual understanding over memorization, especially for formulas involving axis changes. Blindly applying them almost guarantees mistakes.
The midterm really tested speed and precision, but the final is much more manageable—time was longer, and the format closely mirrored the Spring 2023 past paper, especially the series questions. For series, I initially struggled with comparison techniques, but I realized the exams tend to reuse the same few tricks (like trigonometric squeezing or comparing e^x, lnx, and x^n). Even Taylor series, which I never fully grasped, became manageable once I identified the recurring problem types from past papers. Honestly, if you practice strategically and learn to recognize patterns, the exams are far less daunting than the material suggests.
If you just finish the webwork hw, workload will be very low.
But A range still requires extra efforts.

author: Noker
title: Non-L1
comment_content: Easy materials (easier than math 1013), nearly no proofs, it is all about fast and careful mental math (no calculators allowed) and boring computations
comment_teaching:
I’m not here to go through what others have gone through, but I’ll document my journey here.

I am not registered to Dr Wu’s class actually, but because my lecturer, Dr. Liu Chen Kai, taught extremely poorly, with terrible accent, low volume and unreadable notes, I only attended the first 20 minutes of the first class and decided not to see him anymore.

Then, I watched all Professor Wu’s  lecture videos before mid term and did 4 pastpapers. To be honest, the mid term paper wasn’t hard, but there were too much calculations packed into 1.5 hours, which inevitably led to some mistakes, but I’d say eventually it was the mid term that mattered more, as everyone got similar scores in finals (all my friends got around 80-96) as the finals were 3 hours but just a few questions more than mid term, much less challenging (I have one hour left for the finals but no time left for the mid term)

For the finals, as I was busy working on three presentations, I couldn’t watch the videos, and honestly, the series stuff is straight forward, so the finals is actually easier.

Some points to note:
1. Understand what tests to use for what “structure” of the series is, for example, when you see factorial, it must be ratio test.
2. ⁠do pp and actually summarise the fastest way of dealing with each type of question (e.g for Taylor series, it’s always comparing coefficients if the question ask you the nth derivative of the centre (for some n between 3 and 10)
3. ⁠a secret note: for LQ in finals asking about convergence and divergence, it has been 2 convergence and 1 divergence, so maybe that can guide you for a bit.
4. ⁠the concept of growth rate of functions from math 1013 is very important, ln x<< x^a << x! << x^x for infinite x
5. ⁠some subtle stuff you need to remember: the definition of e, squeeze theorem, and the cheat sheet for finals is not completed, because the binomial series is not included, please memorise it yourself.

comment_teaching:
Some claimed that Chiang was terrible because the mean score of his section was the lowest. But more likely it was because some of his students was not competitive enough, as his section was the only one that had quota when I registered.

After actually attending his lectures, I would say his teaching was quite favorable. Pretty clear English, ample notes on the whiteboards and the pace was not hard to follow. He sometimes shared his thoughts on some math topics, and I think people who like maths would find his course informative.

On the other hand, his lectures tended to be a bit theoretical, thus might not contain many things that directly related to questions in exams. Therefore, do search for more practice questions. Sure that his notes are handy, but do not limit yourself to the notes (personal experience, when looking back to my learning process, I think I did not practise enough, and that's fatal to a math course).

Due to unknown reasons, our progress falled behind the tide, and we finished all materials after Taylor Polynomials in no more than two lectures. Life was hard at that time.

The TA's English was, to be honest, not satisfactory. But her problem solving strategies were worth learning.

Volumes and surface areas, work done, infinite series and sequences, maclaurin and Taylor seriesTechniques of integrations- integration by parts, trig substitution, partial fracrtions, average value of a function , polar coordinates, slopes and arc lengths of polar curves  and maybe-maybe nt hopefully not vectors.

comment_teaching:
Going to lectures is pretty useless. Use the tutorial notes. Use the maths support centre once in a while. LECTURES ARE SO USELESS. Organise chemistry tutor and Professor Leonard's videos on YouTube are the real deal for learning the topics. Watch professor Leonard for the topic, do webwork, few days before exams do past papers - you probably won't regret it:)

It's quite annoying when the professor says "no time today, we will cover this segment rest class" and doesn't do it. I stopped going to lectures within 2 weeks tbh. However, amongst all of them, I've heard TK Lam is the best.


I cannot emphasise this enough - watch professor Leonards videos from the start for every topic, make notes of the examples he uses. IT WILL HELP YOUR UNDERSTANDING SO MUCH. AND PAST PAPERS AFTER DOING ENOUGH CONTENT IS THE KEY TO SUCCESS. PRACTICE PRACTICE PRACTICE
comment_grading: the grading wasn't so bad. Midterm makes you feel doomed. Finals is much better. DO all your homework. Grading is descent. The only KEY WORD is practice. practice a lot. Use webworks to practice per topic after learning the content. USE past papers to understand the format of the paper and get the timing right. Pray to god they don't include vectors. PRACTICE PRACTICE PRACTICE PRACTICE PRACTICE........ Midterm is probably going to be tough by the way and it covers 35% of your grade, so doing well on midterms is essential for an A range. For a B range, you can pull up in finals.

author: bwlch
title: As Expected
comment_content:
Other comments should have shown some about the syllabus of the course, I would not repeat here.
For those who have studied M2, the first half of the course which is mainly about integration and its application would be rather handleable because it is more practical.
However, the second half of the course (not including vectors) is more theoretical and require you to have a clear understanding of each concept which may become quite troublesome.
comment_teaching:
Lecture are more for theoretical concepts.
Tutorials are more for practical skills.
Professor Chiang's teaching is acceptable.
Professor Chiang usually provide a theory and give a proof or some examples about that theory. Simple but useful way to deliver rather hard concepts. Taking notes would be helpful such that you can revise the concepts after the Lecture. (To be honest, sometimes find his hand written notes in lectures to be more helpful than those notes he post on canvas)
Although providing the proof may help with understanding the theory, it also cause the paste of the lecture section to lack behind others. Resulting in the whole Taylor Series part (one of the rather difficult concepts) to be covered in like 1 lecture or so.
TA Henry Tsang is pretty good at explaining the concepts that needs to be focused on and is more helpful compare to some other TAs I would say. The tutorial for this sections is mainly doing the worksheet provided which is quite helpful in letting you know how to apply the concepts learned in lectures. You can choose to not come to the tutorial but you would definetely know more on how to tackle questions in exams if you come to the tutorial
comment_grading:
Don't know the grades yet.
Grading scheme:
10% WebWorks (HW 1~9 or 1~10)
35% Midterm Exam
55% Final Exam
Although it saids to follow absolute grading, sometimes it also depends on your classmates or students in other lecture sections.
For the exams, wish that they wouldn't skip any topic such that the question would not vary much from the past paper that they give you for reference.
comment_workload:
To get a good grade some hard work is needed. However, if you are just expecting to pass the course, the workload shouldn't be that heavy. As the instructors have said, they try to make it hard to fail the course.
For the homework, there is about 9-10 for the whole semester and the workload for that would be rather acceptable

author: Tenmark
comment_content:
Including:
Application of integration (Area, volume, work done, arc length, surface area)
Integration (Basic integrals, chain rule, U-substitution,  trigonometric substitution, integration by parts, partial fraction, polar coordinate)
Numerical integration (Left-endpoint rule, right-endpoint rule, midpoint rule, trapezoid rule, Simpson's rule, error estimation)
Improper integral (infinite intervals, discontinuous integrands, p-test, comparison test)
Sequence, series (alternating series, integral test, power series, root test, ratio test)
Taylor series (Newton polynomials, Taylor expansion, maximum/ minimum revisited, L'hopital rule revisited)
Vector (dot product, cross product)
comment_teaching:
Prof Chiang usually spends so much time on going through the proof of some thorems and identities. Just adjust yourself and make your own plan.

Content is the same for every class. MATH 1014 is an extension of MATH 1013.

comment_teaching:
I would say Avery's Lectures are boring and not too useful. He just showed the proof of the theorem and demonstrated some simple examples during his lecture but not much for the calculation technique. Attending his lecture is not useful for concrete understanding. His style in the lecture note is unreadable and hard to understand too because there are many mathematical symbols. However, if you are good in Maths and interested in the proof, you may find it helpful.

I advise the "weaker" students to go to tutorial sessions. The concept and skill will be explained well. Tutors are also easier to approach for asking questions. Moreover, the tutorial exercises are abundant for practice.

BTW, The lecture note written by Dr. Ho Hon Ming is a masterpiece. It explains the concept very clearly with diagrams and provides sufficient examples with step-by-step solution. Make sure to get a copy of it.

A+/A/A- > 85.5 > B+/B/B- >70.5 > C+/C/C- > 50.5 > D > 40

author: zhy
comment_content: The course content is relatively simple and largely overlaps with the standard high school mathematics curriculum in Mainland China. Most concepts—such as calculus fundamentals, algebra, and basic proofs—will already be familiar to students with a STEM high school background. While the material is not challenging, it is presented in a clear and structured manner, making it easy to follow through provided lecture notes and recordings.
comment_teaching: Attendance is optional, and the course is designed to be highly self-directed. Lectures are available in both recorded and note form, allowing students to study entirely independently. Instructors and TAs are generally accessible but are not essential for success. The teaching style is traditional and follows a set syllabus without much deviation, which can be helpful for exam preparation but may lack engagement for some.
comment_grading: Grading is the most competitive aspect of this course. While exams are not particularly difficult, final grades follow a strict percentile-based curve. Only a fixed percentage of students can receive an A, meaning you must not only perform well but also outperform a significant portion of the class. This creates a high-pressure environment despite the straightforward content. Assignments count for only 10% of the final grade, with the majority resting on midterm and final exams.
comment_workload: The workload is light. Assignments are few, straightforward, and low-stakes—some students even use AI tools to complete them without issue. With no mandatory attendance and abundant resources available online, students can manage their time flexibly. However, the competitive grading means that those aiming for top marks must still invest time in thorough exam preparation, even if the weekly commitment remains low.

author: [H_K_U_S_T]
title: Difficult Course
comment_content: The course content was well-structured and highly informative, covering essential topics like applications of calculus, vectors, and sequences. It provided a deep dive into mathematical theories that are fundamental to advanced analysis. However, as a business student who had been away from math for a while, I found the pace and depth quite challenging. I often struggled to keep up because I had forgotten much of the basic calculus required for the course. While the quality of the material was excellent, it may be a bit demanding for those without a very strong or recent mathematical background.
comment_teaching: In my opinion, the teaching style was not particularly effective or engaging. Since it was a large-scale lecture, it seemed difficult for the professor to pay attention to individual students' progress or needs. The delivery of the material often felt impersonal, making it hard to stay focused throughout the semester. On the bright side, the optional tutorials were incredibly helpful and well-organized. The TA did an excellent job of breaking down complex concepts, which was much more effective than the main lectures.
comment_grading: The grading for this course felt quite rigorous, as is often the case with many math-related subjects. Even though I put in a lot of effort, my final exam scores weren't as high as I had hoped. The assessment criteria seemed strict, leaving little room for partial credit or generous curves. It definitely felt like the grading was based purely on accuracy and logical precision rather than effort. Overall, you should be prepared to work exceptionally hard if you are aiming for a top grade in this class.
comment_workload: The workload for this course was quite manageable and not overly burdensome. The assignments were primarily focused on reinforcing what we learned in class, serving as a good way to review the material. It didn't feel like there was an excessive amount of busywork or unnecessary projects. While the content itself was difficult to grasp, the required tasks were mostly designed to help us practice the core concepts. As long as you stay on top of the weekly reviews, the workload shouldn't be a major issue.

comment_teaching: I think his teaching is alright. Delivery is clear. He goes through topics using handwritten notes (they are quite readable for me) and you can revise using the notes.
comment_grading: Don't expect too much from a math course in UST. The grade is 10% on webwork assignments, 35% on midterm and 55% on final. I got 89% overall and got A-. If you took any other session like I did the difficulty of the paper is ok, there's just a lot of concepts to master.

author: 9137dsa18y2
title: L3
comment_content:
US Calculus 2 curriculum, focusing on

Integrals: area/vol, avg value, work, u-sub, trig-sub, t-formula (it's mainly in tutorial exercises tho, it's not explicitly tested in exams), by parts, partial fractions, polar calculus topics

Sequence: Squeeze Thm, typical sequential limit topics

Series: evaluating G.S or telescoping series, different tests for series divergence/convergence, remainder estimate (this appears in WebWork, but not in exams and tutorials), power series and interval of convergence, Maclaurin and Taylor series, series expansions

It's pretty nice to following US curriculum because I can easily find a lot of related youtube videos to help.

author: Free Rider
title: L1 is fun
comment_content:
If you want to study math as a major, highly recommend to take L1, it helps you to understand better in Analysis (Math 2033). You can also join if you are confident in maths. The course gives you a better unstanding in maths and calculus.
(I am an engineering student)

Topics:
- Integrability, Riemann and Darbox sums
- Integration techniques and improper integral
- Vectors and geometry (volume, area, arc length, surface area)
- series (convergence test, power series)
- Taylor series and Fourier series

One part of the course is about computational math, but it's much more difficult and lengthly than non L1 sections, and it may contain multiple cases.
Another part is about proofs. Need to be familiar with proofing skills and theorem such as contradiction, squueze thm, MVT, IVT etc (also those from 1013 L1). The proofs should also be rigorous enough to include all conditions.
comment_teaching: Professor Cheng expians the concepts clearly and in an engaging way. The notes are also well written and provide sufficient examples. The tutorial session also provides useful exercises for homework and exams.
comment_grading:
The exams are generally difficult so think carefully and get prepared before entering.

My score: midterm 83, final 73, homework almost full, final grade: A+
Ability is way more important than effort in this course.

author: Marcuslam
title: Hard
comment_content:
Integration Techniques: More advanced methods beyond basic antiderivatives, such as integration by parts, trigonometric substitution, and partial fractions.
Applications of Integration: Calculating areas between curves, volumes of solids of revolution, arc length, and applications in physics (e.g., work, center of mass).
Infinite Series: This is often the most challenging part. It includes understanding sequences, series convergence tests (ratio, root, integral, comparison), power series, Taylor and Maclaurin series.
Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates: Analyzing curves defined parametrically and in polar form, including calculating derivatives and areas.

author: nickname01
comment_content:
This course is about one-variable calculus. It includes the fundamental theorems of calculus, different methods to compute the volume of some solids, applications of integration, area of surface of revolution, etc. It is more difficult compared to 1013, but it won't be too hard to understand.
Professor choice doesn't matter. More than half of the students in my class skipped lectures and learn it by themselves, and nearly nobody attended the tutorials.
Take this course as early as possible, especially if your major requires a solid mathematics foundation. If you can transfer 1013 and take 1014 directly, I strongly recommend doing this instead of wasting your time in 1013 to get good grades. I think it's better to start learning those more advanced courses earlier.
comment_teaching: The way he taught us was either good or bad. He provided clear structures for every lesson, offered extra exercises in class (which are not graded) to help us have a better understanding of the theorems and the techniques, and was always willing to answer questions. However, he spoke so slowly that it sometimes made it difficult to concentrate on his topic. I've seen many students doing homework during lectures. From my perspective, it's okay to do so for higher efficiency, but you had better preview the content or make sure that you can catch up with the professor when you raise your head.
comment_grading:
The grading system is quite straightforward:
Final 55%
Midterm 35%
Assignments 10%
FYI, I got full marks in assignments and 90+ in both final and midterm. The total was around 94 and I got an A+.
comment_workload:
The workload was bearable. Finishing assignments on time is important. Usually, the assignments were opened a lot earlier than the corresponding content was taught. Pastpapers would be very helpful. You will get a bit busy before the midterm and final, as you have to bury yourself in pastpapers.
Compared to 1013, the workload would be slightly heavier, but I believe it is lighter than most math courses.

author: gleb
title: It ain't that bad bro
comment_content:
Yeah its tough, new content from high school and 1013, but tbh overall it ain't that bad. IF you lock in, study everyday, you'll be alright.  

1. Integral Calculus:
Techniques of Integration:
This includes methods like integration by parts, trigonometric substitution, partial fractions, and numerical integration (like Simpson's rule).
Applications of Integration:
Calculus 2 applies integration to find areas between curves, volumes of solids of revolution (using disks, washers, and shells), arc lengths, and surface areas of solids of revolution.
Improper Integrals:
This involves integrals where the interval of integration is unbounded or the integrand is unbounded.
Average Value of a Function:
Calculating the average value of a function over an interval.
2. Differential Equations:
Basic Concepts: Introducing the idea of differential equations, which relate a function to its derivatives.
Separable Differential Equations: Solving equations where variables can be separated.
Slope Fields: Visualizing solutions to differential equations using slope fields.
Exponential Growth and Decay: Modeling phenomena like population growth or radioactive decay.
Euler's Method: A numerical method for approximating solutions to differential equations.
3. Sequences and Series:
Sequences:
Understanding sequences, their convergence and divergence, and properties like boundedness and monotonicity.
Infinite Series:
Adding up an infinite number of terms and determining if the sum converges to a finite value or diverges.
Convergence Tests:
Learning tests like the integral test, comparison tests, ratio test, and root test to determine convergence or divergence of series.
Power Series:
Representing functions as infinite power series and exploring their convergence properties.
Taylor and Maclaurin Series:
Representing functions using Taylor and Maclaurin series, which are special types of power series.
4. Other Topics:
Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates: Introducing parametric equations and polar coordinates and their applications in calculus.
Vector Operations: Some courses may introduce basic vector operations.
Real-Valued Functions of Two Variables: Exploring functions of multiple variables.

WATCH PROF LEONARD HE'S THE GOAT

comment_workload: Very high, similar to PHYS1112.

author: Joshualization
title: A good professor that will guide you along the way in L1
comment_content:
Calculus (II): Continuing the syllabus of MATH1013, it covers
Integration,
Techniques on Solving Integration,
Some Applications of Integration,
Series,
Taylor Series,
and this marks all the topics included in MATH1014.

However, since I have chosen L1, which the contents are slightly different from normal sessions, it also covers vectors, and Fourier Series.

L1 is more difficult than other sessions, as it focuses strongly on mathematical proofs and reasonings rather than mere calculations. You will learn more about how theorem's are derived, and explore deeper on "why this would happen". If you are a person that is full of curiosity, loves logics, and does not want to only use mathematics like a tool, L1 is suitable for you.
comment_teaching:
Henry's teaching is very good. Clear, concise, and you can immediately find out the main points. For some ambiguous concepts that may sound abstract, just attend his lessons or watch his lecture videos and he could explain them clearly.

However, do watch his videos in sequential order, and do not think that you already understand a specific topic and jump it (for people who is not a math genius like me), because you may not catch up what he had said.
comment_grading:
For L1,

Your weighted score is based on 3 categories:
1) Assignment (20%)
2) Mid term (40%)
3) Final exam (40%)

I don't think that the mid term and final examination are extremely difficult that you cannot even get marks on every single questions, but be prepare that the cut-offs would be low, and many of the questions you encounter would leave unsure or even blanked.

However, the grading is fair, and it just pulls the curve (although the MATH department always tells you they do not). There is around 130-140 people, and I am at the 29th rank. I got an A- eventually. (Both my mid term and final exam are higher than the upper quartile for several marks)
comment_workload:
For L1,

8 Assignments that requires you to submit, which are not webwork but paperwork instead. That means you will need to work for this MATH1014 course at least once a week. Also, you would need around 6 hours work for every assignment.

However, this is surely inadequate for those who want to aim at a good grade on this course. On top of the time you put into assignments, you are strongly advised to delve even more time and effort on practices, extra exercises, and reviewing lecture notes. The time for your self-revision should be even longer than you completing your assignments.

At last you may find out that you are doing maths along the whole semester, but yeah, this is maths.

author: An123
title: Challenging if you are from 1003
comment_content:
I am only here to deliver one message, hoping no one would repeat my mistake.
If you are from MATH1003 who got A- or above, planning to take this course in the following semester,
DON'T do it YET, take 1013 FIRST!!!
Or else you would struggle a lot like I did.
This course and 1013 are sequence courses, meaning prof would expect students already equipped with prior knowledge of Calculus that were never taught in 1003.
I knew I was fked up at the first lesson when prof started "reviewing" caculus concept from 1013. I had to catch upthe theories and formulas I missed in order to catch up with the class.

In case you are wondering, I ended up getting C-, which I am happy with cuz I was initially so worried of getting an F.
The school is so fked up setting the prerequisite being 1003 A- or above when there is apparently a gap in learning progress.
I knew there would be a little gap but I've studied M1 in secondary school so I though I could handle it.
I only got reminded by my friend from 1013 that the gap is quite large on the last few days of the add/drop period...

If you are reading till this point, I hope my story inspired you to think twice, but if you still want to take the course, here are some topics you should catch up first.
1. L'Hospital Rule
2. Trigonometric Functions
3. Differetiation and Integration of Trigonometry
4. Radian with the unit circle method
5. The properties of graphs of sin,cos,tan
e.g. domain, range, period
I'm not a 100% sure the entire knowledge gap would be filled after stuying the above topics so I would also recommend you to go through the materials of 1013 in the winter/summer holiday you are in.

Good luck folks:)

author: 1K2KuQBBF3qCIMt
title: Spring Sem MATH1014 L1 Math Major Section
comment_content:
Midterm 前
Ch5 Integrals
-Area and Integral
-Riemann sum, Darboux sum
-Properties of integrals (e.g. Integral equality&inequality, MVT of integrals)
-Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
呢part難度上無乜特別。

Ch6 Techniques of integration
頭半part: M2嘢(u-sub, by parts, trigo identities)+ t-sub + partial fraction
後半part: Inproper Intergral 加埋佢哋嘅convergence test.
後半part係靠你發現/察覺到個pattern先做到，係要有啲math嘅 sense
先諗到。不過又咁講，呢樣係可以靠操past paper增加吓啲sense嘅…

Mid-term後
Ch7 Vectors and geometry
頭半part: Conic Section(唔太重要), Polar Curve, Euclidian Vectors(Final淨係出咗幾題TF)
後半part: Parametric Equation，用integration搵一個curve嘅arc-length, area, volume同surface area
後半part要背式再加要式將得x,y變parametric equation入面嘅t，唔難但係in起嚟小煩。

Ch8 Series
-Series 加埋佢哋嘅convergence test.
-Power Series同佢哋嘅radius of convergence
同ch6後半part幾似，再加多幾種convergence test，比起ch6後半part再難多D。

Ch9 Taylor series and Fourier series (算係成個course最難嗰課)
-nth degree Taylor approximation
-Evaluate limits using Taylor polynomials
上面呢兩part考試出得好Mechanical，就算你唔明佢做緊啲咩，都係照喺formula Sheet度抄返落嚟再爆開
-Taylor series
  -Lagrange’s remainder formula
-Fourier series
  -Basel Problem
D concept對於數學個sense差少少嘅人會比較難get
Fourier Series嘅concept係幾複雜(當然Taylor Series其他都係)，不過考試前只要記住嗰條式同埋Basel Problem個應對方法，應該點都執到D分。

不負責任評論：其實我覺得數底/sense好嘅人讀L1應該係會靚grade過讀其他section. 所以就算唔諗住math major但係數底幾好嘅人，其實都可以試吓讀L1。
