author: Kelvin70211
title: MATH 1014
comment_content:
Difficulty & Learning Curve
Easiest segment: Vector basics and parametric curves (if you are comfortable with geometry).

Steepest learning curve: Infinite series tests (p-series vs. comparison, alternating series test, ratio/root tests) and Taylor error estimates.

Common pitfalls: Mixing up convergence tests, misapplying separation of variables, and under-practicing improper integrals involving comparison.
comment_teaching:
Lectures emphasize conceptual explanations first, then worked examples; many instructors use visualizers or short CAS demos for curves and series.

Tutorials/recitations focus on problem-solving drills and past-paper style questions; some sections add Maple/Matlab labs for visualisation.

Support includes office hours and an online discussion forum (Piazza/Canvas), but proactive students who form study groups benefit the most.

Teaching quality can vary, yet most lecturers succeed in connecting abstract tests for convergence with intuitive pictures (e.g., stacking blocks, spring motion).
comment_grading:
Typical breakdown (can shift ±5 % by instructor):

Component Weight Notes
Weekly homework (WebWork / written) 25 % Mix of routine and proof-lite questions; lowest score often dropped.
Mid-term test 20–25 % Weeks 5–6; covers integration applications & vectors.
Quizzes / labs 5–10 % Short checks; may be online or in-person.
Final exam (comprehensive) 40–50 % Heavy on sequences/series & Taylor series.

Grades are usually curved to a departmental target, with ~20 % earning A range. Partial credit is generous for correct setup, especially on series and ODE items.
comment_workload:
Time investment: 6–8 hrs/week for solid competence; 9–12 hrs/week to aim for an A, mostly on series drill and ODE modelling.

Homework arrives weekly and can be long—expect 12–18 multi-part problems, plus optional challenge questions.

Study rhythm: Work steadily; the steepest curve is Week 9–11 (convergence tests). Leaving series practice to revision week is a common pitfall.

Pace intensity: Manage overlapping submissions—problem sets, mid-term prep, and lab reports often cluster around Weeks 5–7 and 11–13.

author: GolfGTI
title: Don't be intimidated by Calc II
comment_content:
TL;DR
MATH1014 is more like a puzzle than mathematics. You actually need to think and get your brain running to do well in MATH1014.

Quote taken from https://tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/CalcII/CalcII.aspx
(very useful online notes which I relied on)

"Calculus II tends to be a very difficult course for many students. The [main] reason many students have difficulty with Calculus II is that you will be asked to truly think in this class. [...] You can’t just memorize a bunch of formulas and expect to pass the course as you can do in many math classes. There are formulas in this class that you will need to know, but they tend to be fairly general. "

The calculation aspect of MATH1014 is not as heavy as other courses. Since you can't use calculators in exams anyways, you are usually expected to answer in terms of pi, e, natural logarithms or exponents. Rather, the hardest part of MATH1014 is the thought process. Analyzing the question and THEN deciding what technique to use is usually the challenge in most questions. For example, we are introduced to many tests for the convergence of an infinite series, but some of them are more effective than others for a particular series. The ratio test is effective for factorials, the root test is effective for n^n, the comparison test is effective if you know a convergent/divergent series from the get-go (e.g. harmonic series is divergent) etc. Finding the value of an infinite series takes up a small portion of the series part of the curriculum; instead proving its convergence/divergence is more common. In the case of definite integrals, once you know the technique, substituting the upper and lower bounds is usually very easy, and terms like sin(2pi), tan(pi/4), ln(e) usually appear, which turns into simple numbers (if you know your Calc I).
comment_teaching: Teaching is alright. Prof. Lam is good at math but only so-so in his teaching as his notes can get a bit messy (please get him some correction tape). What I recommend is copying at least 80% his notes during lectures, as I believe the act of writing it down etches the knowledge into your brain 10x more effective than just taking a picture of the lecture notes and forgetting about it.
comment_grading: Grading is alright. There are gods that can get really freaking high scores in exams, but if you know your Calc I, M2 or MATH1012 well enough then you should have a satisfactory grade.
comment_workload: You should definitely do all your homework in time because it takes up quite a big portion of the grades and they are usually decently easy (or WolframAlpha your way through if you are really struggling, but take it from me - always try your hardest to solve one question before resorting to WfA, or use WfA for the final answer, and get the steps nailed down by yourself).

author: whysoserious
comment_content:
This course serves as an introduction to the fascinating realm of one-variable calculus, marking the second installment in the MATH1013 – MATH1014 sequence. It is designed for students eager to expand their mathematical knowledge and skills. The curriculum encompasses a variety of essential topics that are crucial for a comprehensive understanding of calculus.

Students will explore the applications of definite integrals, which are fundamental in calculating areas under curves and solving real-world problems. Improper integrals will also be covered, providing insights into cases where traditional integral methods may not suffice.

Additionally, the course will delve into the study of vectors, curves, and parametric equations, allowing students to visualize and analyze geometric concepts in a more dynamic way. The modeling of real-life situations through differential equations will be another key focus, equipping students with the tools to describe complex phenomena mathematically.

As they progress, participants will engage in solving simple differential equations, gaining practical skills that are applicable in various scientific and engineering fields. Furthermore, the course will introduce students to the intriguing world of infinite sequences and series, including the exploration of power series and Taylor series, which are vital for approximating functions and understanding convergence.

Through this comprehensive approach, the course aims to prepare students not only for further studies in mathematics but also for practical applications in their academic and professional pursuits.

comment_teaching: The quality of instruction in MATH1014 is debatable. While the professor possesses both knowledge and enthusiasm for the subject, they merely offer a general overview of the course and fail to provide substantial support to students.
comment_grading: Grading in the A range starts at 80+, which is reasonable. The course difficulty is moderate, truly demanding robust integration abilities, and the questions asked are direct. The final was acceptable, though it excluded vector topics because the professor taught at a slower pace. The last two series of multiple-choice questions were very much a case of knowing it or not. The long questions followed a conventional format, including one question on justifying convergence or divergence, another on integration (involving surface area or volume), and there were some proof questions, though another question has slipped my mind.
comment_workload: One webwork assignment is given each week, which overall serves as a useful tool to verify understanding of the concepts. Although some of the questions can be quite challenging, it's manageable since you have unlimited attempts before the deadline.

author: ync
title: practice makes perfect
comment_content:
Application of Integration: This section covers topics like finding the area between curves, calculating volumes, determining work, and computing arc length. If students are familiar with the relevant formulas and can make the connection between real-world problems and integration techniques, this part of the course is generally manageable.

Techniques of Integration: This section delves into various integration methods, including integration by parts, trigonometric integrals, trigonometric substitution, partial fractions, approximate integration, and improper integrals. To become proficient with these techniques, students will need to do a significant amount of practice, as the use of these methods tends to be the most challenging aspect of the midterm exam.

Polar Coordinates: This topic is relatively straightforward if students can familiarize themselves with the necessary formulas and their applications.

Infinite Sequences and Series: This broad topic encompasses sequences, series, techniques for testing series, power series, and the representation of functions as power series. To excel in this section, students will need to dedicate ample time to practicing the different methods for testing the convergence or divergence of series, as this tends to be the most confusing part of the final exam.

Vectors: This is a basic introduction to vectors and is not included in the homework or exams.
comment_teaching:
It can be challenging to fully assess TK's teaching approach, as the effectiveness may depend on each student's individual learning style and needs. However, there are some notable aspects to consider.

In every lecture, TK arranges a review of the previous class material, which can help reinforce key concepts. When introducing new topics, he makes a concerted effort to thoroughly prove and explain the underlying concepts and formulas, with the intention of ensuring clarity.

For students who appreciate the process of rigorous mathematical proofs, attending TK's lectures can be a worthwhile experience. However, due to the time constraints, TK is only able to provide relatively simple example problems, which may feel too easy compared to the more complex questions encountered on exams.

This discrepancy between the lecture examples and the assessment questions has been described as a "boiling frog" effect - the gradual ramp-up in difficulty catches some students off guard. Therefore, if students are aiming for high grades in the course, they will likely need to invest significant time in supplementary practice and problem-solving beyond what is covered in the lectures.

While TK's lecturing style may have its limitations, the tutorials led by Phyllis seem to be more impressive and beneficial. It is highly recommended that students attend Phyllis' tutorial sessions and attempt the exercises she provides, as this can help bridge the gap between the lecture content and the level of understanding required for success in the course.
comment_grading:
The assignments in this course can be manageable if students collaborate with their peers and supplement their learning by searching for information online. However, this approach should be balanced with individual effort and understanding.

The midterm exam is relatively concise, consisting of 10 multiple-choice questions and 2 longer-response questions. While the questions may not be overly complex, they do require students to apply their knowledge and critical thinking skills.

The final exam, on the other hand, is more comprehensive, with 15 multiple-choice questions and 3 longer-response questions. This exam tends to be more challenging, particularly the questions related to testing series, which many students find difficult compared to the sample materials provided.

The grading scheme for this course is clear but strict. The final grade is determined by a weighted combination of 10% homework, 35% midterm, and 55% final exam. The grading scale is set as follows: A range is >85%, B range is 70-85%, C range is 50-70%, D is 40-50%, and F is <40%.

The course is designed with a target mean score of around 50% for both the midterm and final exams, which translates to a grade in the C range. This can be a source of frustration for students aiming for higher grades.
comment_workload:
There are a total of 9 homework assignments in this course. For students who are simply aiming to pass the class, completing the homework to a satisfactory level can be a viable strategy.

However, for students seeking to earn a good grade in the course, simply relying on the homework assignments may not be sufficient. The homework problems tend to be relatively straightforward and may not adequately prepare students for the more challenging questions that appear on the exams. It is highly recommended to invest substantial effort into completing additional practice problems and exercises beyond the homework assignments.

author: kypang
comment_content:
One of the notable aspects of this math course was the slow pace of the lectures. While some students prefer a slower pace to fully grasp the concepts, it can be a disadvantage for those who have a solid foundation in mathematics or prefer a more accelerated learning experience. The slow pace may result in boredom or a feeling of time wasted for such students.

Due to the slow lecture pace, many students found themselves seeking alternative learning resources to supplement their understanding of the course material. One popular recommendation was to watch YouTube clips on specific math topics. These clips provided concise explanations and demonstrations, which allowed students to learn at their own pace and review the material at their convenience. The availability of online resources like YouTube has undoubtedly revolutionized the way students approach their studies, offering a wide range of perspectives and teaching styles.
comment_teaching: Contrary to the popular belief that the tests in this math course were exceptionally challenging, I found them to be relatively manageable. The long questions, which often involved integrations, were straightforward if one had a solid grasp of the different integration techniques. Recognizing and applying the appropriate method to solve these questions was the key to success.
comment_grading: The tests in this math course were primarily concept-based rather than relying heavily on computational skills. This approach aimed to assess students' understanding of the underlying principles and their ability to apply them to various problem-solving scenarios. While this format may be challenging for some students who prefer more straightforward calculations, it encourages a deeper comprehension of the subject matter.
comment_workload: The course heavily utilized webwork, an online platform for submitting math assignments and quizzes. While webwork can be challenging for some students, there was an abundance of online resources available to assist in completing the assignments successfully. These resources included websites, forums, and video tutorials that provided step-by-step explanations and examples for each problem type. The availability of such resources proved invaluable in helping students navigate through the webwork assignments and clarify any confusion they encountered.

author: paburon
title: Best professor for ones who barely passed MATH1013
comment_content:
More calculus (mainly integrals)
Polar coordinates
Sequences and Series

comment_teaching:
For a student who barely passed MATH1013, I was afraid i would not be able to pass this course. But professor Wu breaks down and simplifies each topic, so it was very easy to follow and understand the content. She introduces various types of problems, and each show the calculation process step by step. So you can basically have an example guide for most questions you may encounter in homework assignments and in exams.
Her lecture slides are also very neat and detailed, so you can easily review and understand even without the class recordings, unlike some other professor's lecture notes.

However, finding a good tutorial session might be difficult, since most TA's either cannot teach, or have an uninterpretable accent.
comment_grading:
Grading is absolute grading, so you just receive based on how well you did on the midterm and final.

The avg grade for this course is a C+, but note that there are about 1200+ students taking this course. I think you wont get a bad grade if you can generally understand the content before the finals.

Midterm - 35%
Final - 55%
Homework - 10%

8. Mid-semester examination (26thSunday morning). Infinite series; Integral test (16); Comparison test; Alternating series (17)
9. Absolute convergence, Ratio and Root tests (18); Power series (19)
10. Representation of functions as power series (20); Taylor and Maclaurin series;  Applications of Taylor polynomials (21);
11. Vectors (22), Dot product (23);
12. Cross product and applications (24)
comment_teaching: The professor teaches at a comfortable pace, making it easy to follow along. He frequently utilizes the whiteboard, so make sure to sit towards the front of the class in order to see the words clearly. Attendance is not mandatory, as he uploads all material, including written notes, onto Canvas. Additionally, he enjoys going beyond the curriculum, delving into topics like hyperbolic functions. If you're interested in this kind of material, I would highly recommend taking his course.
comment_grading:
The grading system is quite tough. It follows an absolute grading scale with slight adjustments every semester. However, if you do poorly on the midterm, even if you do well on the final exam (nearly 100%), it may not necessarily lead to an A range grade. This semester's midterm was especially challenging, particularly the final question. Unfortunately, I lost 20 marks on this question because I had trouble connecting parts a, b, c, and d.


Assessment

0% Worksheets (Done in tutorials)

10% WebWorks

35% by Mid-semester exam

55% by final exam

Rough divisions: A+/A/A- > 85.5 > B+/B/B- >70.5 > C+/C/C- > 50.5 > D > 40
comment_workload:
To succeed in this course, it is important to complete the online WeBWork exercises on a biweekly basis. The good news is that you have infinite attempts, so you should be able to achieve full marks. While reviewing past papers can help you familiarize yourself with the content, it's important to remember that they don't necessarily reflect your performance on exams.

Overall, staying consistent with the exercises and engaging with the material will help you excel in this course.

author: kckck
comment_content:
Typical lectures. Top down teaching. No attendance required.
Typical tutorials. Short recall of taught content and solve related problems. No attendance required.

Covers a lot about integrals and series, such as integral approximations, and convergence and divergence of series and sum of series. Ends with vectors, which is purely M2 stuff, but it wasn't covered in the final exam as other professors could not manage to go through the topic.
comment_teaching:
Accent might be a bit to heavy to local students, but I personally think it is fine. She is good relatively when compared to other professors. Notes are provided on canvas, so you do not have to worry much about missing out any info in class. They are also very useful for revision purposes.

I know there are other professors who use whiteboards (physical ones) and students have to copy everything frantically to make sure they have everything in their notes before the professor clears the whiteboard anytime soon.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading.

Midterm more difficult than final. Grading scheme was changed after the midterm to allow the exclusion of midterm results, accounting for 90% of the total grade. Remaining 10% from assignments.

Assignments same as MATH1013, unlimited attempts for assignments with the support from the math support center if needed. As long as you do them you can get full marks for the assignment part.

overall score of about 83-84% is already enough for A range.
comment_workload: Acceptable, an assignment every 1 to 2 weeks on average. Usually between 10 to 15 questions per assignment. However, there may be questions with parts a, b, c, d... so the workload fluctuates from week to week. Easy ones require 1-2 hours, while difficult ones can take up to 8 hours to complete.

author: dkfsdfnn

comment_content:
- Integration techniques: fundamental theorem of calculus, substitution rules, trigonometric identities and trigonometric integrals, integration by parts, partial fractions, numerical integration, etc..
- Improper integrals: evaluation, convergence and divergence problems.
- Applications of integrals: areas, volumes by slicing and cylindrical shells, arc lengths and areas of surfaces of revolution, work done, average value of a continuous function, calculus in polar coordinates.
- Infinte sequences and series: limit of an infinite sequence, convergence and divergence an infinite series, geometric series, various convergence/divergence tests, alernating series.
- Power series: interval of convergence and radius of convergence of a power series, Taylor and Maclaurin series of functions, operations of power series, such as adding, substituting, differentiating or integration power series.
- Vectors (WAS NOT IN FINAL DUE TO CORONA SEMESTER)

author: Twcheng
comment_content:
If BDSM is your thing, then you'll enjoy this. You get to learn all sorts of ways to torture yourself you didn't even know existed!!! From the light and kinky stuff like volumes of revolution to the hardcore and masochistic stuff like series expansions and partial fractions expansions.

Okay, in all seriousness, it's Calculus 2 for those who follow the US curriculum. You learn integration techniques for the first half then you go on to learning series, sequences etc in the second half. I'm sure it's useful stuff, but still hard none the less.
comment_teaching:
Extremely difficult to understand what he's saying. He has about a 1:1 ratio of filler words to actual words when "teaching". So when he teaches it goes something like this "uhh, so uhhh, today we will ummm, be talking about uhhh integration by errrr parts. To uhhhh integrate........" When I heard that on the first class, I knew that I was in for a very bad time.

Basically non-existent teaching. Instructor blitz through the content like there's no tomorrow. If you go to class, you can only hope to learn something through osmosis, because you'll be furiously taking notes that you'll never read/won't understand or you'll give up half way and just start playing with your phone or flirting with the girl next to you. Which you can then flex your intelligence and show her how much you've self learned.

To rub salt into the wound, you get NDS students who seem to understand everything to the point where they can even correct the instructor's careless mistakes. fml. If they're that good, why even go to class??
comment_grading: Absolute bullshit. You get essentially a bi-modal distribution where you have us double digit IQ locals getting 30/100 and you have the 10% of students who say that this course was a breeze and get 90+/100 for everything. Mid term and final was a bloodbath
comment_workload: The only redeeming thing about this course is the workload. The only continuous assessment that is given is the infamous webwork. However, the webwork gives you unlimited tries so you can slowly guess your way through each assesment which takes only about 1.5-2 hours each on average. Or you could Wolfram Alpha that shit and be done in 15 minutes if you don't give a damn about learning the material.

comment_teaching: Fairly speaking, CHING Avery is a very enthusiastic professor, and he spares efforts in teaching this course. He will provide the typed course notes after every lecture. comment_grading: The grading..... I think is not good at all. We have 10 more homework on ClassViva, and it's easy for all of you to get full marks. However, there is no midterm this semester and we have only one final exam. But the final exam is designed in a very bad way: 20 multiple choices and 2 hours time. Fairly speaking, if you do your daily works well, it's easy for you to get 90+ or even 100. As I know lots of students get 100. Too light!

Like 1013 you are forced to study on your own, I highly recommend Khan academy and professor Leonard on youtube if you want to study, especially for professor Leonard since he uploads FULL lectures on everything taught in this course with VERY clear explanations, and native English.

Here is his youtube channel: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCoHhuummRZaIVX7bD4t2czg
comment_grading:
25% WebWork, 75% Online final

The online final: 20 MC questions, open book, which is nice as you don't have to memorize the formulas and instead focus on understanding the formulas, it made the finals easier.

However, the finals is STILL quite difficult. Especially if you are a M1 student like me, however I would consider the exams to be easier than 1013.

For the homework, its entirely possible to get full mark on every webwork since you can google the questions (even if you don't have chegg), if you are willing to spend the time its very doable, you also have infinite attempts, so you can spam and brute force some answers (if you are REALLY struggling).

Overall I got full marks in the homework and 65/100 in the finals, and got a B-, fair grade considering I was only 2-3% higher than the mean.
comment_workload:
Most of the time spent on this course would be learning the course contents yourself, since the lectures are useless.

As for the WebWork and studying, webwork isn't too difficult, since you can Google almost EVERY question, even if you don't have Chegg like me, you can still find simliar questions that would help you figure out how to do the questions.

Of course you would need to study for the Finals, and that takes a few days to prepare for, do the past papers and you would have a general sense on what to expect.

comment_teaching:
Dr Chiang is a decent teacher who is willing to explain concepts and tries his best in delivering the content to us via the whiteboard. comment_grading:
Nothing speical about the grading as it should be absolute grading; however, MCQs are weighted very heavily given that the paper is out of 100 and each MCQ counts for 6 marks. No partial marks is given for MCQ, so it is very important to be very familiar with the content taught in class to get them right.

Remember to review exam papers after getting them graded as there could be mistakes in marking. Tutors are however fabulous at explaining webworks.

Final grade: MIA as finals are still to be taken.
comment_workload: WebWork once a week; questions cover everything taught in class. author: Fairyfire
title: Straightforward and Fair
comment_content:
Syllabus:
Basic integrals: e.g. area between curves
Volumes by cylindrical shells
Arc length and area of a surface
Integration by parts, trigonometric integrals
Trigonometric substitutions and polar coordinates
Partial fractions and strategy for integration
Approximation integration and improper integrals

Midterm

Sequences and series, integral tests
Various tests for convergence and divergence
Testing series, power series
Taylor and Maclaurin series
Applications of Taylor polynomials
vectors in three-dimension
Dot product, cross product

Policy:
15% Homework(Classviva)
35% Midterm
50% Final

Basically, materials before midterm are simple and do not need the effort to handle, but the contents after the midterm become a bit hard and require some self-practice. Fortunately, both the midterm and the final exam are really easy, especially for the final exam which is even easier than the sample paper.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Wang's organizes the lecture notes very well and her pronunciation is pretty ok. You may feel the class would be a bit tedious, but overall Wang's teaching is quite good. She explains concepts clearly and will give us lots of examples. She also has review notes which are quite helpful before exams.
Also, she is willing to answer students' questions.
comment_grading:
The grading is fair I guess.
The following scheme is from the syllabus:
Provisional grades:
100%≥A+/A/A-≥85%>B+/B/B-≥70% >C+/C/C-≥50%>D≥40%>F

I don't have the information about the curve within a subgrade, but it's highly possible to get both the midterm and the final full marks, so don't worry.
comment_workload:
Not much. If you hate maths, then do not take this course for any other reason since you will feel awful for the calculations.
According to my friends, this course is much simpler than math1024 and also has a better grading scheme. So if you are not a math guy, go ahead to take it and enjoy the satisfaction of solving questions.

author: Androe
title: 盡情刷龜吧
comment_content:
一開始講各種定積分的應用很簡單，理解了就好。
後來講換元積分分部積分啥的，每次把講義認真地看懂，每道例題都要做。然後同濟高數上的題都做一遍。日常準備一個錯題好題本，考試前刷兩遍，滿分不是問題。
後半期講各種approximation，稍微抽象一點點，但老師的講義都很好用的，基本上講義和作業題吃透了就OK。最後期末比其中會難一點點，但只要不粗心滿分也沒問題。
平時作業很簡單，尤其是Classviva可以一遍一遍刷成績，而且現在題目不會改數字了，刷個兩三遍都是滿分。
comment_teaching: 一堂課都沒去過，不知道講課咋樣。但都很認真在準備講義，很易懂，很詳細，very useful。聽說有些其他堂的講授講義非常簡略，這一點上WANG Ke老師真的很棒。
comment_grading:
好像是絕對龜吧，詳見Content。
最後：Homework 15/15 + Midterm 35/35 + Final 48/50 = 98/100，拿了A+。
comment_workload:
正常workload，比honor堂不知好到哪裡去了，詳見Content。
這門課其實學習方法很簡單，把講義吃透，手上能找到的題都做一做，時常整理知識點和錯題，好龜沒問題。

author: Nayr
comment_content:
Integration and application of integration
Integration techniques
Power series
Vectors
comment_teaching:
Purpose of attending lecture is to know the teaching progress. Basically, if you are not confident with the content in this course, you can attend the tutorial session or do more exercise and practice more.
Avery's style of teaching is not suitable for me, there is no powerpoint during the lecture, just hand-copied notes from Avery. He will post it to the course page a few hours after the lecture. Therefore, it is optional whether you are going to copy the notes during lecture or not.
My tutor is Mr. Ho Ming Leung, his tutorial notes are nice and will give each theory an example question and full solution. There is additional exercise at the last page of the notes. However, I don't really know why the answer to additional exercise is not provided.
comment_grading:
Mid-Term exam: 30%
Final exam: 55%
Webwork:15%
Bonus quiz 8%
SD is large this semester, it is not absolute grading in this semester. However, you must get at least 40% of the course grade to get a pass in this course.
The mean for MT and Final is around 40% of the total mark because the performance of midterm is really poor so that Avery introduced another grading scheme which allowed students to omit their mid-term score. However, I think the final exam is more difficult than midterm so better work hard and try to score a higher mark in the midterm exam.
comment_workload:
There is only 11 HW this semester, which is around 1 webwork homework per 1.5-2 weeks.
4 Bonus quizes can save many people's grade.
Basically, you got plenty of time to do exercise on your own. My advice is to get familiar with the question first.

comment_content: 基本上學過M2不會有太大難度,進階微積分,但如果沒有學過/只學過M1必學努力. Midterm前不太難,但midterm後就不能走堂,否則必定跟不上. 不能考前3-4日先溫書

comment_content:
Basic mathematics concepts required for science, engineering students. Most of them were taught in M2. It is an advantage if you take M1/M2 in your secondary school.

Most of the syllabus is about differentiation and integration. You also need to learn how to calculate the areas and the volumes. Vector is also taught in the course.
comment_teaching:
Lectures teach the concept and tutorials teach the skills in answering questions. Professor Lam Tsz Kin is a bit boring but you can easily understand his speaking if you understand Cantonese. He will cover all the syllabus but also include some out-of-syllabus knowledge in the lectures.

For the tutorials, LIANG Shixin is a good TA since she provided many teaching notes for students. She will teach the concept again in tutorials and answer your questions comprehensively. Even if you only go to the tutorials, you can still understand the concepts. She also provides some questions for students to teach the answering skills. Some of the questions are similar to the questions in examination.
comment_grading:
The course is graded in absolute grading. For this semester, the grading is about like this:

85-100: A
65/70-85: B
50-65/70: C
40-50: D
0-40: F

For the marks,
Webwork (15%)
Midterm exam (35%)
Final Exam (50%)
Bonus quizzes (8%)

It is difficult to fail the course if you complete all the webwork and the bonus quizzes, and they are not difficult to complete if you can find others to carry. The course is said to be very difficult for the past few years, but for this year, the final exam paper is very similar to the sample paper and many people get high marks.
comment_workload:
No attendance.
One midterm and one final.
4 bonus quizzes for you to improve a sub-grade, so please complete them.
Webwork is a bit annoying, but finishing them can ensure you understand the concepts well. These 15% can also hep you a lot.

author: K. H.
title: Practical Math Course
comment_content:
Although this course is designated as the subsequent course of MATH 1013, the focus of this course is more on computations and less on proofs. Even though there are some proof questions appear in the exam, you just need to memorise all the methods mechanically, unlike in MATH 1013 where some proofs requires your Math maturity. Moreover some skills in this course, such as definite integral and its application, Taylor's expansion, series and its convergence e.t.c., are actually very useful in further studies. So I recommend people who are from science and engineering school, regardless of their future major, should work harder in learning the materials.

One side note is that concept of vector is introduced at the end, which I believe people who studied M2 in high school should be rather familiar with. However for other students, such as those who studied M1 in high school, this would be a chance to learn vectors as a prerequisite of more advanced math courses, such as linear algebra.
comment_teaching:
Many has already commented Dr. Ho's teaching so I am not going to repeat. It is worth notice though that if you wish to choose Mathematics as your major (especially those who wish to choose pure maths track, though I would expect you be studying MATH 1024) , you might also want to sit other Professor's lecture or learn from additional reference book because his teaching many focus on computations and the transition from theory to application, which is not that helpful in developing math maturity and learning develop different concepts rigorously.

Einstein Kong is also a very helpful TA and he is also good at explaining abstract concepts. So do try to sit his tutorial (if there is).
comment_grading: In this semester, the Professors claimed that absolute grading scheme is used though I suspect grading curve is also employed. I am not in a very justified position to comment this so I shall refer to other comments for this section.
comment_workload: It really depends on your previous knowledge on calculus and your math maturity. M2 really helps reduce a lot of workload. However even if you haven't studied M2 before, you can theoretically avoid some workload as the assignments (WebWork) are rather trivial that you can find answers on the internet. Though this will probably cost your grade if you don't really understand how to solve the assignments. Bonus quizzes are useful in increasing sub grades or even avoiding failing this course so it is worth to try. You can refer to sample quiz and the actual quiz is about the same with number differences.

author: Anisha
comment_content:
The amount of content is very reasonable for the length of the semester provided that you provide consistent effort throughout. Once you slack, it gets super difficult to catch up and the exam week becomes a nightmare. You need to put a decent amount of effort in solving past papers and tutorial notes because the sums in the exams are similar but a lot more challenging. If conceptual understanding is not attained, forget even about a B.

NOTE: IF ANYONE NEEDS CLEAR SOLUTIONS TO MOST OF THE TUTORIAL NOTES SUMS, FEEL FREE TO HIT ME UP!
comment_teaching:
Avery's lectures were basically lessons on how a simple formula or relationship was derived which is completely useless when it comes to doing well in exams. He will direct questions to the students and end up answering them himself almost immediately lol. He has a very thick accent which gets a few weeks at least to get accustomed to and by the time you do, you realize half of the semester has gone by and you have better things to do than decipher the lecturer's accent and that's when you start running to the MSC, desperate for help that you don't even seem to know where.

On the other hand, Phyllis is an excellent TA. The way she will explain concepts to you are exemplary. She will ingrain them in your mind and make sure you understand fully before moving on. However, she has a slight accent problem but it is way easier to comprehend than Avery's.

All in all, I'd recommend skipping the lectures but following the tutorials religiously.
comment_grading: Pass and fail is absolute but the final grade is curved depending on the mean and SD. In Fall 2017, the mean for the midterm was about one-third of the full mark and for finals, it was less than 50%. That is how badly people score. And since midterm and final weighs about 33% and 55% respectively, you're screwed.

author: keith Wong
title: MATH1014
comment_content:
If BDSM is your thing, then you'll enjoy this. You get to learn all sorts of ways to torture yourself you didn't even know existed!!! From the light and kinky stuff like volumes of revolution to the hardcore and masochistic stuff like series expansions and partial fractions expansions.

Okay, in all seriousness, it's Calculus 2 for those who follow the US curriculum. You learn integration techniques for the first half then you go on to learning series, sequences etc in the second half. I'm sure it's useful stuff, but still hard none the less.
comment_teaching:
Extremely difficult to understand what he's saying. He has about a 1:1 ratio of filler words to actual words when "teaching". So when he teaches it goes something like this "uhh, so uhhh, today we will ummm, be talking about uhhh integration by errrr parts. To uhhhh integrate........" When I heard that on the first class, I knew that I was in for a very bad time.

Basically non-existent teaching. Instructor blitz through the content like there's no tomorrow. If you go to class, you can only hope to learn something through osmosis, because you'll be furiously taking notes that you'll never read/won't understand or you'll give up half way and just start playing with your phone or flirting with the girl next to you. Which you can then flex your intelligence and show her how much you've self learned.

To rub salt into the wound, you get NDS students who seem to understand everything to the point where they can even correct the instructor's careless mistakes. fml. If they're that good, why even go to class??
comment_grading: Absolute bullshit. You get essentially a bi-modal distribution where you have us double digit IQ locals getting 30/100 and you have the 10% of students who say that this course was a breeze and get 90+/100 for everything. Mid term and final was a bloodbath
comment_workload: The only redeeming thing about this course is the workload. The only continuous assessment that is given is the infamous webwork. However, the webwork gives you unlimited tries so you can slowly guess your way through each assesment which takes only about 1.5-2 hours each on average. Or you could Wolfram Alpha that shit and be done in 15 minutes if you don't give a damn about learning the material.

author: smiling
title: Strongly recommend the MATH 1014 lecture taught by Dr HO, Hon Ming
comment_content:
Around 30-40% content are taught in M2 syllabus.

1) Applications of Integration
- Areas between two curves*
- Volume by slicing (Disk Method)*
- Volume by cylindrical shells (Shell Method)
- Length of curves
- Area of a surface of revolution
- Physical applications (mass, density, work done)

2) Integration Techniques
- Basic integrals*
- Integration by parts*
- Trigonometric Integrals*
- Trigonometric Substitution*
- Method of partial fractions
- Numerical Integration
- Improper integrals

3) Polar Coordinates
- Polar coordinate system
- Areas in polar coordinate system
- Arc length in polar coordinate system

4) Infinite sequences and series
- Sequences
- Infinite series
- Divergence test & integral test
- p-series
- Ratio, root & comparison test
- Limit comparison test
- Alternating series test
- Absolute convergence of infinite series
- Taylor polynomials & Taylor series
- Power series

5) Vectors*
- Three-dimensional coordinate system*
- Dot product*
- Cross product*

Content above put with asterisk are in M2 syllabus.
comment_teaching:
Dr Ho teaches by 3 screens. He writes down what he says while teaching, so you must know what he is talking about and you can follow the progress. He will repeat the important point once. The notes written in the lecture actually come from the the notes you got, but you should still attend his lecture, you will understand it better. At the end of each lectures, he will ask all students whether they have questions, I have never seen that there was students raising questions at that moment. However, if you really have questions, you can and you should ask him after the lectures. He is willing to answer you. You will feel his solemn attitude in the lecture and I also noticed his smile once only in the lecture (same as one of the previous reviewer). However, you will notice his sincere smile more if you ask him questions sincerely.

Two reminders :
You need to attend his lectures to get the lecture notes. There are lecture videos on Canvas.
comment_grading:
Grading scale : absolute grading
A+/A/A- >= 85% > B+/B/B- >= 70% > C+/C/C- >= 50% > D >= 40% > F

Grading scheme :
15% by Online homework assignment (Webwork)
35% by Mid-term exam
50% by Final exam
+8% by 4 additional Online Bonus Quizzes

Mid-term mean : 52.09   SD : 15.54
Final mean : 67.34   SD : 15.29
Total mean (excluding bonus quizzes) : 66.77   SD : 11.59

One thing to highlight is that the final mean is higher than the mid-term mean, this implies that the difficulty of the final is even lower than that of the mid-term! The final exam is indeed easier in this semester.

My results :
Assignment full marks
Mid-term : 59 (above 0.44 SD)
Final : 87 (above 1.29 SD)
Bonus quizzes : 96.07% (+7.69%)
Overall (including bonus quizzes) : 86.84%
Got A-
comment_workload:
Main workload is the online homework (Webwork). Better to finish the relevant questions after the that content was taught. If you want to get a better result in the exams, you should review the lecture notes on the day that being taught. Don't accumulate these little workloads until the exams, you will be overwhelmed by the thick lecture notes. I
suggest finishing some tutorial exercises.

author: GK
title: More than M2, less than what a 3 credit course should do
comment_content:
This course continue from the MATH1013, starting with integration. The method of integration is mostly the same as of HKDSE M2. However, partial fraction was not included in my DSE education, which is included in this course. (But some other students said that this was taught also in M2)
The application of integration is more difficult than HKDSE M2. After that, sequence and series are also being taught. This might be quite difficult for M2 students so don't think this course is as easy as MATH1013. The content can be said to be less than  what a 3 credit course should be, but it is not as simple as MATH1013.
comment_teaching:

The lecture notes are excellent and his class is better than Ho Hon Ming. Don't understand why he is less popular but I think it is quite fun to attend his class.
Dave Au is an excellent tutor. He would try his best to answer student's question.
comment_grading:
This course is a standard bad grade course. The mean is somewhere on C+ (?). I got 9X in final with above mean 1 SD at midterm, resulting in A only. Very pissed off.

comment_workload: Super low if you have M2 basic. All you need to do is to do easy webwork and self study for exams. Most part of the course can be ignored if you have M2 base. Take this course as a vacation course!

author: aple
title: Good course. No attendance required.
comment_content:
-Syllabus:
Topics are the same as other comments. They are basically chapter 5-8, 10-11 in James Stewart’s calculus textbook(some sections are excluded). The lecture ppts are pretty much identical to the textbook as well (at least for my lecture/instructor). Bits of chapter 12 (vectors,dot/cross product) is covered in lectures too, but they’re not tested in exams.

-Format:
Since lectures and tutorials are not mandatory to attend, you can just read the ppt lecture/tutorial slides by yourself.  (Best part about this course) I personally read the textbook instead of the lecture slides.
There is also WeBWorK homework (online homework)

-Exams
The long questions in exams are more difficult than the questions in online homework, but the multiple choice questions are pretty much the same as the online homework. I’d say just try to understand the concepts and do your best in the exams.
comment_teaching: I only attended 2 lectures because the professor basically reads the ppt aloud with her own elaboration, and the ppt is pretty much identical to the textbook. This isn’t “bad”. It’s just that there’s no point in attending lectures when I can just read the textbook. If your schedule is tight or you don’t want to spend much time reading textbook, then her lectures are useful.
comment_grading:
10% webwork homework
35% midterm exam
55% final exam
No attendance required.
comment_workload: Not high compared to most other courses. You have to finish online homework about every week/2 weeks. Each problem set has around 20 questions. Infinite attempts, so if you keep doing it, you will eventually get full mark or close to full mark.

author: Neo Lee
title: Kind and Patient Professor (Rewarding Learning Experience) (not L1)
comment_content:
This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus, the second in the MATH1013 - MATH1014 sequence. Topics include applications of definite integral, improper integrals, vectors, curves and parametric equations, modeling with differential equations, solving simple differential equations, infinite sequences and series, power series and Taylor series.

Tricky parts: Integration by Parts, Taylor Series.
Overall: not very hard.
comment_teaching:
His teaching is good. He tries his best to ensure that everyone grasps the concepts. He is very open to questions. This is what I love about him most. If you have some confusion, don't hesitate to raise it in class. You can also contact him after class if you have any questions.

He writes his notes on Notability instead of PowerPoint. Maybe you should consider whether this handwritten style is suitable for you. But one's style may change over time. I recommend you audit his class during the Add and Drop period before you make your final decision.

comment_teaching:
Due to my decision to not attend lectures, I am unable to provide a personal evaluation of this specific lecturer. However, I can share that the course materials are provided in the form of handwritten notes and in-lecture notes, which allows for self-learning.

Additionally, attending Phyllis Liang's tutorial sessions is highly recommended. Her tutorial notes are of high quality and serve as excellent practice material. Reviewing her tutorial notes before attempting past year papers can be more efficient than going through all the lecture notes. It's worth noting that her tutorial sessions tend to fill up quickly, so it's advisable to arrive early to secure a seat.
comment_grading:
The difficulty ranking for the course components is as follows:
1. Lectures: Considered the least challenging aspect of the course.
2. Homework: Slightly more difficult than lectures, a bit useless I would say.
3. Tutorials: Some tutorial questions are taken from homework and past year papers, making them moderately challenging.
4. Past papers: More challenging than tutorials as they provide a comprehensive review of the course material.
5. Actual Exam: The most difficult component as it covers the entire course and requires comprehensive understanding.

It's important to note that since the grading is absolute, your performance in the course will solely depend on your understanding and preparation.
Letter grades will be assigned depending on overall performance. The following is the rough guideline: A+/A/A- > 85.5 > B+/B/B- >70.5 > C+/C/C- > 50.5 > D > 40

Homework (WebWork) 10%
Midterm Examination 35%
Final Examination 55%

9. Power Series and Taylor Series* (radius of convergence, Maclaurin expansion)
(the most difficult topic I think)
10. Vectors (dot product, cross product,

The topics with * are important topics (tested a lot in the final exam)

The content is really difficult. The syllabus of DSE M2 only covers a very small part (by part, disk method) of the content of this course. Some of the topics like Reimann sum in MATH 1013 can be seen in this course.

comment_content:
- knowledge in Math 1013

- work (physics)

- polar coordinates and calculus

In the final exam, the main focus is on the series and sequence. Requirements of and conclusion given by each test (for convergence or divergence of a series, eg Comparison Test, Alt. Series Test, ...) should be RECITED clearly. It is also reminded that if requirements of a test are not completely satisfied, usually no conclusion can be drawn.

There is a distinct teaching and exam style for those lectures taught by Dr Hu Jishan. His exam and test are different from others in that they focus on proofs whereas others focus on application matters.

It could be a great advantage if you took M2 in college.

Mr Fan is a patient and responsive TA, despite few students' attendance of tutorial class. You can ask him after tutorial class, or by email. There was once just some hours before the exam that he replied to my email only 6 minutes after the email was sent to him.

comment_teaching: Overall acceptable. TJ's teaching is generally well among the instructors of math department. He always has a clear thinking process and explains concepts clearly. However, I have to say that his lecture notes, which contain straightforward explanations of concepts and numerous examples,  are even better than his teaching. Actually, if you look through his lecture notes carefully, it is not essential for you to attend the lectures.
comment_grading:
Since there were bonus quizzes(8%) this semester, the grading was decent. But there are some differences in different semesters.
Grading scheme: 15% webwork + 35%midterm+ 50%final +8% bonus quizzes (4 quizzes in total)
Webwork: since there are unlimited attempts, I think you should try your best to get full marks since the questions are not that difficult.
Midterm: Difficult due to a tight time limit. You have to complete 11 MCs and 3 long questions in 1.5 hours, which is extremely challenging. However, if you are clear with the concepts and do enough exercise before the exam, it is high possible for you to get a satisfactory score.
Final: I think it is much easier than the midterm since the time limit is 3 hrs but only 4 MCs and 1 long question are added. If you are careful enough in the final exam, you are able to get a high score.
Bonus quizzes: difficulty and format similar to webwork. If you review the lecture notes carefully before taking each quiz, I think full marks are not difficult.
To conclude, if you do enough exercise after classes, A range is not difficult, but A+ needs some more efforts.
comment_workload: Overall moderate. 10 webworks in 13 weeks (but 1 in the midterm break, you have to remember the due date in case that you miss it). You just have to review the lecture notes before the exams, but if you grasp the concepts and formulas well, it won't take you too long.

author: B.C.
title: Finally a math course that encourages learning
comment_content:
- Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
- Area and Volume (Disc and Shell methods)
- Integration by Parts
- Trigonometric Integration and Substitution
- Vectors, Dot and Cross Product

------ New content (DSE M2-speaking) ------
- Work (Physics)
- Average value of function
- Arc length and Surface
- Polar coordinates, Polar Arc length and Surface (Parametric Equations)
- Partial fraction and its integration
- Numerical methods (Approximate Integration)

- Sequence and Series
- Series Tests
- Alternating series
- Power series
- Taylor and Maclaurin Series

- Set definition of planes, intersection of planes (slightly mentioned only)
comment_teaching:
Li Zhen is a new professor, teaching for his first time.

- Merit: Passionate and actively listening. His lecture style is just to use the whiteboard for everything, very wonderful as we can follow how he thinks. His presentation skills in terms of delivering the content is spectacular, everything just links together as lecture approaches the end: you can see him trying to show a theorem many times with various examples as if until you are convinced. Feel free to keep him after lecture for questions and you would see him solving them with you. (Handwritten notes available later online)

- Weakness: His English accent is not particularly good but understandable. His pace of going through concepts and examples in a lecture can be quick and even accelerating, but it is equally satisfying if you manage to follow such a pace; there is often a 5 minute break in the middle :)


Zeta Chan, our tutorial TA, is very practical in solving questions. More like exam-oriented style problem-solving, which is interesting because Prof. Li put it theoretically and you may yet see how you can apply things. A big thumbs-up for his tutorial question set: he stapled and prepared a collection of all-topic questions across the whole semester, just bring that over every time and go to the question he is doing now (answers in a file was released online later). Questions are also highly welcomed; he would walk to you to confirm if you did it right, either by asking or looking at your writing. His English carries the Hong Kong-er accent but you can tell despite this he lays out things intuitively.


TA support in the Math Support Center is sufficient but usually poorly delivered. I don't feel welcomed, but rather kind of a trouble asking obvious questions. Maybe the TAs are already annoyed with meticulous queries of simple questions, and they are systematic enough to put up solutions onto the whiteboard just letting us to refer to it. But regardless of how much we learnt, this is not a learning environment that encourages trying, at least to me.
comment_grading:
15% Webwork - basically full mark for everyone
35% Midterm
50% Final
* +8% Quiz bonus (4 quizzes each 2%) - special policy in my semester because our (all students taking math 1014 this semester) midterm results are too horrible in general

Absolute grading:
- Overall 85% -> A-range hence A-
- (Seems Overall 90% -> A)
- (Seems Overall 95% -> A+)
So the quiz bonus is really a lenient policy. In my case I was just off A-range and got an B+, but because of the quiz results I got two sub-grade up to obtain an A in the end
comment_workload:
Webwork: treat it as a concept revision, look up the concept after you try to do it yourself, then it won't seem difficult; do it early so that you can seek help when you figured out you need

Midterm and Final: definitely attempt the sample paper given, because often the format is similar. Tutorial exercises are recommended as revision materials before final, because merely understanding would not be enough. You can even get tutorial exercises from other tutorial sessions, for example I used: Einstein's https://sites.google.com/site/einsteinkongstutorialwebsite/

(Quiz: in this semester we have the quizzes starting from Easter break. 4 quizzes split into two periods to take, two quizzes available each period.
There were sample questions available for you to exercise with for as much time as you wish, and frankly the format is again very similar to the real quizzes.
Recommended to take them all early because as the deadline approaches the Math Support Center would be full-house but you must take the quiz with the computers there not yours; the quiz also has a time limit of 15-25 mins, so taking it with fewer people around shall relieve stress if any)

For math lovers or curious people: I made notes solely on equations/concepts on my own and did the in-lecture/tutorial examples together with the lecturer/TA. So in my humble opinion, paying more attention during lecture and tutorial plus some little reinforcements at times would save much time. It is both more rewarding and efficient.

author: Lui
title: It is not a good course
comment_content: This course, known as Calculus 2 in the US curriculum, is notoriously challenging. The curriculum is divided into two demanding halves: the first focuses on advanced integration techniques, and the second delves into the complex topics of series and sequences. While the material is undoubtedly valuable, its difficulty is significant and requires considerable dedication to master.
comment_teaching: Actually, Some instructors note are good. But Extremely difficult to understand what he's saying, It can be challenging when some peers grasp the material so quickly that they identify and correct errors in real-time. While their insight is impressive, it sometimes makes the learning environment feel intense. I'm working on viewing it as a chance to see the material from a different perspective, though it does highlight the varying levels of prior experience in the class
comment_grading: NO GOOD. ME C- But at least no D.
comment_workload: The manageable workload is one of the course's stronger aspects. The primary form of continuous assessment is a series of online problem sets. A key feature is the unlimited attempts allowed, which encourages a practice-oriented approach. This means you can spend time genuinely engaging with the material—typically 1.5 to 2 hours per set—to solidify your understanding through trial and error. While it's technically possible to use external tools to quickly find answers, doing so ultimately undermines the deep learning the assignments are designed to foster.

author: xgordonyyy
comment_content: The course covers essential calculus topics like integration techniques, sequences/series, and multivariable calculus, providing a solid foundation for STEM majors. The material is rigorous but well-structured. Some students find the transition from single-variable to multivariable calculus challenging, though the syllabus aligns with standard undergraduate curricula.
comment_teaching: Quality varies by instructor. Some professors explain concepts clearly with real-world examples, while others focus heavily on theory, which can confuse beginners. Lecture notes are usually detailed, but supplementary resources (e.g., step-by-step problem-solving demos) would help. Office hours are valuable for clarification.
comment_grading: Assessments include homework, midterms, and a final exam. The problem sets are fair but exams can be tough, with time pressure and tricky questions. Partial credit is given, but the curve depends on the cohort’s performance. Averages tend to be moderate (~B range), but top grades require deep understanding.
comment_workload: Manageable but demanding. Weekly problem sets take 5–8 hours, and mastering concepts requires consistent practice. The pace accelerates mid-semester with new topics like Taylor series and double integrals. Group study is recommended to tackle harder problems.

Needless to say, the 18th of May was Judgement Day.

With each minute that passed in the exam hall, I could see fire rain down from the heavens and smite the poor souls, one by one. Given the conditions of the exam, it is highly unlikely that anyone would pass. I knew for a fact that I would fail. But I didn't.

Why?

There is only one possible explanation.

Ten days after the final exam and a few days before results were released, a divine tradegy occurred.

He died.
Almost noone noticed at first.
Reading the news gave me shivers.
After a while, I felt different.
My soul felt cleansed, my mind clear.
Boys and girls seemed worriless and carefree.
Everyone seemed.. purified.

He died for our sins. I will be forever grateful.

Rest in peace, our beloved savior.

author: Mubin
comment_content:
L01 section is harder than other L02-11 sections and easier than MATH 1024 and it is specifically designed for potential Math Majors. While the main content is similar to other sections, L01 section emphasizes conceptual understanding and will have a lot of proof writings.
If you enroll in other sections, you may find it harder to transit into second-year math courses, especially MATH 2033 - Mathematical Analysis, which is a required course for Math majors, and it requires a strong logical mindset and solid conceptual understanding of single-variable calculus.


(Note that, unlike other sections, L01 section starts from the beginning of integration.)

Integration: Riemann Integrations, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Integration Techniques, Reduction Formula, Improper Integrals.
Geometry: Vectors, Parametric Equation, Polar Coordinates, Volume.
Sequence and Infinite series: Convergence Tests, Power series, Taylor Series, Fourier Series.
comment_teaching: Prof. Ivan's teaching pace is always like watching a video at 1.5x speed. To grasp all the content, it's better to study the lecture notes before attending the lectures. It will also be great if you watch the recording of the lecture again after attending the lecture in person.
comment_workload:
You have to solve a lot of problems. You have to dedicate a lot of time to that. Solve ALL the problems of Calculus, Early Transcendentals on Classviva. Make sure you keep in mind the different type of problems available there. Some of them will be trivial, harder problems will come in handy. Make sure you solve the Exercise problems of Lecture Notes, Worksheet Problems and go through the tutorial notes.

Finally, DO NOT be a deadline fighter!

DO ALL PASTPAPERS YOU CAN FIND. CLASSVIVA QUESTIONS ARE
comment_teaching:
Disclaimer: I am in a good mood today, this review is likely biased in TK's favor. (On a regular day, will give him F)

Teaching Style:
TK likes to go into a lot of theory not useful for exam. He also enjoys making up questions on the spot that he cannot solve without making 2+ mistakes. Very hard to follow and connect theory/proofs to questions solved, especially as they are not similar to exam or homework style questions and he does not explain thought process before solving + always makes mistakes.
Often, he just explains and derives the origins of each formula/shortcut mathematically but allocates very little time to
how and when to use each formula.

Time:
Spent too much time re-capping 1013/high school content that we were left with 3 lessons before finals for the final topic. TK decided to not test on final topic but notified everyone with 1 week before the exam. He also kept teaching the final topic and did not go over previous content at all (in SBM it is common for professors to have 1-2 revision lessons to refresh our minds before finals season)

TK's unpreparedness in lessons meant that he did a lot of improv. He makes up questions on the spot and sometimes decides halfway through solving it that it is too tedious and changes the question - but doesn't demonstrate how to solve it! (He only gave a general/theoretical explanation). He also makes a lot of mistakes that students have to point out. Would hence recommend rewatching recordings instead of joining live.
To buy time for improv, he writes down everything he says on his iPad. Whereas TAs have prepared condensed, no BS version of curriculum and some even have handwritten scans of questions, solutions, diagrams etc (like PPT style teaching) and explain things more thoroughly. Highly recommend watching tutorials (fortunate for me, zoom Uni meant I had access to all TA tutorials in 2 lectures so I could choose the one that suited me most)
comment_grading:
General performance in midterm so poor that a grading scheme was introduced MIDTERM(35%) + FINAL(55%) and purely FINAL(90%) grades, grade is best of 2.

Fair grading by UST standards, but less generous than other SBM courses. 62.97% mean, got B for 77.5%
comment_workload:
Homeworks per subject section on Classviva. Knowledge required is approx 2 weeks behind the teaching schedule, so can finish even if you are behind on lessons.

Can be tricky but not unreasonable. Aim for 100% because unlimited tries + ample time = insanely high mean

author: yu006493
comment_content:
Week 1 Review of definite integrals and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, area between curves, volumes by cross-sections.
Week 2 Volumes by cylindrical shells, work.
Week 3 Average value of a function, arc length, area of a surface of revolution.
Week 4 Integration by parts, trigonometric integrals.
Week 5 Trigonometric substitution, polar coordinates and calculus.
Week 6 Partial fractions, summary of integration techniques.
Week 7 Approximation integration, improper integrals.
Week 8 Infinite sequences and series, integral test.
Week 9 Comparison tests, alternating series, absolute convergence, ratio and root tests
Week 10 Power series, representation of functions as power series.
Week 11 Taylor and Maclaurin series, applications of Taylor polynomials, two and three-dimensional coordinate systems, vectors.
Week 12 Dot product.
Week 13 Cross product.

The correlation between each weeks topic is not big, you can still able understand the content even if you didn't catch up the previous lecture.

I don't like this course because this course emphasizes computation more than proof/understanding, in mid-term you just have to compute lots of definite integrals again and again and calculator is not allowed. You need to be fast and careful.

author: abcbca
title: Frustrating Math course.
comment_content:
It's a consecutive math course of MATH1013. The content of this course mainly include 5 general topics:

(1) Basic Knowledge of Integration
Substantial content is included in M2.
- Integration of simple functions
- Integration by Substitution
- Integration by Parts
- Trigonometry + Integration

New Concept:
- Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration
- Distance travelled by particle
- Future Value and Net Change

(2) Application of Integration
Half of the content is covered by M2, but the exam question can be extremely difficult without using calculator.
- Area between curves
- Volume of solid by revolving around axis/line i.e. Disk/Shell method

New concepts:
- Slicing Method
- Arc Length of a curve
- Surface Area of the solid generated by revolving around axis
- Hooke's Law
- Density and Work
- Polar Coordinates and Calculus
- Area bounded by curves and Arc length of curves in Polar Coordinates
- Partial Fractions and Integration
- Estimation for the value of Finite Integration
- Improper Integrals

(3) Sequence and Series
Important content:
- Limit of a sequence
- Partial Sum, Sum of Series
- Geometric Series
- p-Series
- 8 Convergence Test of Series

(4) Application of Series
- Taylor/Maclaurin polynomials
- Binomial polynomials
- Remainder Theorem of Taylor Series

(5) Vectors
All concepts are covered by M2.

Personally, I think the most difficult part of the course is Work and Comparison Test.
comment_teaching:
I skipped all lessons and tutorials.

I studied hard on half of all lecture notes on Canvas, especially Work, Sequence and Series.

Notes of Professor Zhang are treasures. If you deliberately revise on all the notes, you should obtain a clear concept.

Remember that all proves will not be examined.

Midterm

Actually, if you want to get a normal/good grade, the quizzes must be done as almost everyone would choose to do it and get (nearly) full marks. Don't consider the quizzes as a tool to redress for the marks in your exam.
comment_workload:
If you haven't taken M2 as your elective in secondary school, you must pay double efforts to get a better grade as 50% of the course content is bounded in the syllabus of M2, including around 70% of the Integration topics and all Vector topics. You must pay more efforts to it as a student who has studied M2 may not even be able to handle this course easily.

If you didn't study anything about Physics in secondary school, make sure you've understood and prepared for the questions about Works under the Integration topic. It is quite likely to appear in Mid-term and Final exams.

If you've studied M2 in secondary school, Congratulations!
You have relatively less workload than others, but you've to pay extra attention to Convergence tests for Series as many students failed to understand and apply in the exam questions. The series to be tested in those questions can be unimaginable.

author: Haozhe Kuang
title: Good Grading for AP Students
comment_content:
MATH 1014 是微积分课程的第二部分，涵盖以下主题：  
- **积分的应用**：曲线之间的面积、体积（使用切片法和圆柱壳法）、弧长、表面积和功。  
- **积分技巧**：分部积分、三角积分、三角替换、极坐标和部分分式。  
- **反常积分**：处理无界积分。  
- **无穷级数和序列**：收敛性测试（比值、根式、比较和交错级数测试）。  
- **幂级数和泰勒级数**：函数的幂级数表示、泰勒多项式及其应用。  
- **向量**：二维和三维向量的运算，包括点积和叉积。  

MATH 1014 is the second part of the calculus course, covering the following topics:  
- **Applications of Integration**: Area between curves, volume (using slicing and cylindrical shell methods), arc length, surface area, and work.  
- **Integration Techniques**: Integration by parts, trigonometric integrals, trigonometric substitution, polar coordinates, and partial fractions.  
- **Improper Integrals**: Handling unbounded integrals.  
- **Infinite Series and Sequences**: Convergence tests (ratio, root, comparison, and alternating series tests).  
- **Power Series and Taylor Series**: Power series representation of functions, Taylor polynomials, and their applications.  
- **Vectors**: Operations with 2D and 3D vectors, including dot product and cross product.
comment_teaching:
课程通过讲座和辅导课进行教学。讲座主要在LTE或CYT-LTL举行，辅导课则在不同的教室进行。教师和助教提供支持，通过CANVAS系统分享课程材料，并在数学支持中心提供一对一辅导。（Tutorial一定要去，非常有用，助教讲解考试难题）

The course is taught through lectures and tutorials. Lectures are mainly held in LTE or CYT-LTL, while tutorials are held in different classrooms. Teachers and teaching assistants provide support, share course materials through the CANVAS system, and provide one-on-one tutoring in the Math Support Center. (Tutorials must be visited, they are very useful, and the teaching assistants explain the exam problems)

Overall: 85/100 (A)

3. Final exam: 55%.
(Difficulty varies from person to person, for reference only)
I am an AP student. If I can get a score of 5 in AP Calculus BC, this course is not difficult because many contents overlap:
My score:

课程每周安排约 4 小时的讲座和辅导课。学生需要完成在线作业、期中和期末考试，并可能需要额外的自学时间来掌握复杂的数学概念。由于课程内容丰富，学生需要保持良好的学习习惯和时间管理能力。  （建议阅读托马斯微积分，考前认真完成Past Paper巩固解题方法）

The course consists of approximately 4 hours of lectures and tutorials per week. Students are required to complete online assignments, midterm and final exams, and may need additional self-study time to master complex mathematical concepts. Due to the rich content of the course, students need to maintain good study habits and time management skills. (It is recommended to read Thomas Calculus and complete the Past Paper carefully before the exam to consolidate the problem-solving method)
